---
title: "Lyme Disease Differential Gene Expression Re-Analysis"
subtitle: "Re-analysis of Bouquet et al. 2016 (GSE63085) using DESeq2 & clusterProfiler"
author: "Abed & Laszlo"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 3
    toc_float: true
    code_folding: hide
    theme: flatly
    highlight: tango
    fig_width: 10
    fig_height: 7
---


# Introduction

In this notebook, we re-analyse the publicly available RNA-seq dataset GSE63085 from Bouquet et al. 2016, which profiled peripheral blood gene expression in Lyme disease patients at multiple time points (acute infection, post-treatment, and 6-month follow-up) compared to healthy controls. The original study used: 

* outdated hg19 reference genome, 
* TopHat2/Cufflinks2 for alignment and quantification, and 
* Limma/Voom for differential expression analysis, with pathway enrichment via the proprietary IPA tool.


Lyme disease is a tick-borne infection caused by *Borrelia burgdorferi*. It can lead to a range of symptoms, from acute flu-like illness to chronic fatigue and joint pain. Understanding the host immune response at the transcriptomic level can provide insights into disease mechanisms and potential therapeutic targets.



# Summary of the re-analysis Implementation

## How Our Re-Analysis Compares to the Original Study

| Aspect | Bouquet et al. 2016 | Our Re-Analysis |
|--------|---------------------|-----------------|
| Genome | hg19 (2009) | GRCh38.p13 (current) |
| Aligner | TopHat2/Cufflinks2 (deprecated) | Kallisto (modern) |
| Input data | FPKM (normalised) | Raw integer counts |
| DE method | Limma/Voom | DESeq2 (negative binomial) |
| Fold-change | Raw FC > 1.5 | Shrunken LFC (ashr) |
| Pathway tool | IPA (proprietary, 3$) | clusterProfiler + MSigDB (open-source, free) |
| Pathway methods | ORA only (1st gen) | ORA + GSEA + GSVA + SPIA (1st–3rd gen) |
| Per-sample resolution | No (bulk group comparison) | Yes (GSVA scores per patient) |

### What we confirmed

- A strong immune/inflammatory signature at V1 (acute Lyme)
- The signature persists beyond antibiotic treatment (V2)
- Partial recovery by 6 months (V5) but not to baseline
- TNF-alpha, interferons, complement, and IL-6 signaling are central

### What we added

- **GSEA** detects subtle coordinated pathway changes without
  arbitrary cutoffs, finding more significant pathways than ORA alone.
- **GSVA** provides individual patient pathway scores, allowing us to
  see heterogeneity within each time point -- particularly relevant for
  understanding why some V5 patients still have elevated inflammatory
  pathways while others recover fully.
- All tools are open-source and reproducible (vs. IPA).
- **SPIA (3rd gen)** highlights KEGG pathways where both **gene list enrichment** and **signed topology** suggest activation or inhibition — a different lens from MSigDB Hallmark.



```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center"
)
```

# 1. Environment Setup

## 1.1 Install Packages

```{r install-packages, eval=FALSE, include=FALSE}
# install.packages(c("tidyverse", "pheatmap", "RColorBrewer", "ggrepel",
#                     "gridExtra", "viridis", "ashr"))
# if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE))
#   install.packages("BiocManager")
# BiocManager::install(c("DESeq2", "clusterProfiler", "msigdbr",
#                         "enrichplot", "org.Hs.eg.db", "GSVA", "SPIA"))
```

```{r load-libraries}
# if (rstudioapi::isAvailable()) {setwd(dirname(rstudioapi::getActiveDocumentContext()$path))}
options(width = 200)

# use this function to install and load packages
using <- function(pkgs) {
  for (pkg in pkgs) {  # Loop through each package in the provided vector
    if (!requireNamespace(pkg, quietly = TRUE)) {    # If not installed, install it
      install.packages(pkg, repos = "https://cloud.r-project.org")}
    # Load the package:
    library(pkg, character.only = TRUE)}}

using(c("tidyverse", "pheatmap", "RColorBrewer", "ggrepel", "corrplot", "gridExtra", "viridis", "BiocManager","DESeq2", "org.Hs.eg.db", "clusterProfiler", "EnhancedVolcano","msigdbr", "PCAtools", "GSVA", "enrichplot", "ashr", "matrixStats"))

# SPIA is Bioconductor-only (3rd-generation pathway topology); not on CRAN
if (!requireNamespace("SPIA", quietly = TRUE))
  BiocManager::install("SPIA", ask = FALSE, update = FALSE)
suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(SPIA))

# description of libraries:
# tidyverse       - data wrangling + ggplot2
# DESeq2          - differential expression (negative binomial model)
# clusterProfiler - pathway enrichment (ORA, GSEA)
# msigdbr         - access to MSigDB gene sets
# enrichplot      - enrichment result visualizations
# pheatmap        - heatmaps with clustering
# RColorBrewer    - colour palettes
# ggrepel         - non-overlapping text labels on plots
# gridExtra       - arrange multiple plots
# viridis         - colour-blind-friendly palettes
# matrixStats     - fast row/column statistics

theme_set(theme_bw(base_size = 13)) # this is for the plots 
```


We had to use explicit `dplyr::` prefixes on key functions (`filter`, `select`, `count`, `mutate`, `arrange`) throughout this document because
`clusterProfiler` loads packages that mask some of these names.


## 1.3 Directories

```{r directories}
project_dir <- getwd()
data_dir    <- file.path(project_dir, "Data")
results_dir <- file.path(project_dir, "results")
plots_dir   <- file.path(project_dir, "plots")

for (d in c(results_dir, plots_dir))
  if (!dir.exists(d)) dir.create(d, recursive = TRUE)
```

---
---

# 2. Data Loading

The raw FASTQ reads from GEO (GSE63085) were re-aligned by Thomas to the modern **GRCh38.p13** human reference genome (replacing
the outdated hg19 used in the original 2016 study) and quantified into raw integer counts, the proper input for DESeq2. Thomas used Kallisto alligner. And more importantly, the 22 missing samples were excluded during re-alignment, will be checked and confirmed by Thomas. 

**References:**

- Bouquet J et al. *mBio* 2016; 7(1):e00100-16.
- Thomas's book T. *Beginner's Handbook of -Omics Data Analysis*, Ch. 7-8.

## 2.1 Raw Counts Matrix

```{r load-counts}
counts_raw <- read.delim(
  gzfile(file.path(data_dir, "GSE63085_raw_counts_GRCh38.p13_NCBI.tsv.gz")),
  row.names = 1, check.names = FALSE
)
cat("Counts matrix:", nrow(counts_raw), "genes x", ncol(counts_raw), "samples\n")
```

## 2.2 Gene Annotation (featureData)

This maps NCBI Entrez Gene IDs to gene symbols, biotypes, chromosomal locations, and GO terms.

NCBI Entrez as the "Google Search" engine for biological data. It’s a massive integrated system managed by the National Center for Biotechnology Information (NCBI) that links together different databases—DNA sequences, protein structures, and medical literature (PubMed).
According to online sources, when we see an Entrez ID (like those numbers in your matrix), it’s a unique "social security number" for a gene. Names (Symbols) change as scientists argue, but Entrez IDs stay the same.

```{r load-gene-annot}
gene_annot <- read.delim(
  gzfile(file.path(data_dir, "Human.GRCh38.p13.annot.tsv.gz")),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)
cat("Gene annotation:", nrow(gene_annot), "x", ncol(gene_annot), "\n")
```
> Explanation of the columns (source: online):

* **GeneID**: This matches the row names in your counts matrix. It’s your primary key.

* **Symbol**: The "human-friendly" name (e.g., TP53, BRCA1). This is what you’ll put in your final paper or report.

* **Description**: A long-form name of what the gene actually does (e.g., "tumor protein p53").

* **EnsemblGeneID**: Another ID system (starting with ENSG...). Bioinformaticians often use both Entrez and Ensembl because some tools prefer one over the other.

* **GeneType**: Usually, you only want to analyze protein-coding genes. Most researchers filter out the pseudo (dead "fossil" genes) and ncRNA (non-coding) unless they are specifically looking for them.

* **Status**: You should likely filter for "active". A "discontinued" status means the gene was merged into another record or found to be a mistake in the genome mapping.

* **ChrAcc, ChrStart, ChrStop, Orientation**: These are the "Location" columns. They tell you exactly where the gene sits on which chromosome and which way it's facing. This is vital if you want to see if a treatment affects a whole neighborhood of genes (a "cluster") or just one.

* **GOFunction / GOProcess / GOComponent**: "GO" stands for Gene Ontology. These tell you the "job" of the gene. **Process**: What is the "big picture" goal? (e.g., "Cell Division"); **Function**: What is the specific biochemical task? (e.g., "Binding to DNA"); and **Component**: Where does it happen? (e.g., "Mitochondria").


```{r meta_EDA}
names(gene_annot)

cat("Status unique:");print(table(gene_annot$Status))
```
> We have ~39k total entries, but only 19,416 are protein-coding. 

## 2.3 Sample Annotation

```{r load-sample-annot}
sample_annot <- read.csv(
  file.path(project_dir, "sample_annotations.csv"),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)
```

## 2.4 Align Samples

22 samples from the annotation are absent from the counts matrix.
The reason is not confirmed -- ask Thomas whether they were excluded
during re-alignment or simply not processed.

```{r align-data}
shared     <- intersect(colnames(counts_raw), sample_annot$GSM)
only_annot <- setdiff(sample_annot$GSM, colnames(counts_raw))

cat("Shared:", length(shared), "| Not in counts:", length(only_annot), "\n")

counts <- counts_raw[, shared]
annot  <- sample_annot %>%
  dplyr::filter(GSM %in% shared) %>%
  dplyr::arrange(match(GSM, shared))

stopifnot(all(colnames(counts) == annot$GSM))
```

## 2.5 Engineer Metadata Columns

We derive three columns from the raw `Observation` string:

- `patient_id`, the numeric patient identifier embedded in the observation name (e.g. `LymeP-12_V1` → `12`). Parsed via two stacked `gsub()` calls so both `_V1/V2/V5` and bare `_V` formats match.
- {`visit`: [`V1`, `V2`, `V5`]}, or {`Control`}.
- `group`, the four-level factor used as the DESeq2 design: `Control / Lyme_V1 / Lyme_V2 / Lyme_V5`.

The patient-ID parser is fragile by nature (it depends on the exact observation naming convention from GEO), so we add a hard sanity check immediately after: every Lyme sample must yield a *purely numeric* patient ID. If GEO ever changes the format, the check fires here rather than producing silently wrong patient trajectories in the PCA (Section 8.3).

```{r engineer-metadata}
annot <- annot %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    patient_id = gsub("^.*-([0-9]+)_V.*$", "\\1",
                      gsub("^.*-([0-9]+)_V$", "\\1", Observation)),
    visit = dplyr::case_when(
      grepl("V1", Observation) ~ "V1",
      grepl("V2", Observation) ~ "V2",
      grepl("V5", Observation) ~ "V5",
      TRUE                     ~ "Control"),
    group = dplyr::case_when(
      disease.state == "Healthy controls" ~ "Control",
      visit == "V1" ~ "Lyme_V1",
      visit == "V2" ~ "Lyme_V2",
      visit == "V5" ~ "Lyme_V5"),
    condition = ifelse(disease.state == "Healthy controls", "Control", "Lyme"),
    group     = factor(group,     levels = c("Control","Lyme_V1","Lyme_V2","Lyme_V5")),
    visit     = factor(visit,     levels = c("Control","V1","V2","V5")),
    condition = factor(condition, levels = c("Control","Lyme"))
  )
rownames(annot) <- annot$GSM

# Sanity check: every Lyme patient_id should be purely numeric.
# If GEO ever changes the Observation format, this will fail loudly here
# instead of producing silently wrong patient trajectories downstream.
lyme_pids <- annot$patient_id[annot$condition == "Lyme"]
stopifnot(
  "patient_id parsing produced NAs for Lyme samples"      = !any(is.na(lyme_pids)),
  "patient_id contains non-numeric values for Lyme samples"= all(grepl("^[0-9]+$", lyme_pids))
)
cat("patient_id parser OK:",
    length(unique(lyme_pids)), "unique Lyme patients across",
    length(lyme_pids), "Lyme samples.\n\n")

cat("Samples per group:\n")
print(table(annot$group))
```

---
---

# 3. Gene Biotype Overview

```{r gene-biotypes}
gene_annot_matched <- gene_annot %>%
  dplyr::filter(GeneID %in% as.numeric(rownames(counts)))

biotype_counts <- gene_annot_matched %>%
  dplyr::count(GeneType, sort = TRUE) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(pct = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1))

ggplot(biotype_counts %>% dplyr::filter(n > 50),
       aes(x = reorder(GeneType, n), y = n, fill = GeneType)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(n, " (", pct, "%)")), hjust = -0.1, size = 3) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Gene Biotypes in Counts Matrix (GRCh38.p13)",
       x = NULL, y = "Number of Genes") +
  scale_fill_viridis_d()
```

About half the genes are **protein-coding** (~49%).
The rest are non-coding RNA, pseudogenes, etc. According to online sources, protein-coding genes carry the strongest biological signal for differential expression. \n 
After filtering (Section 6), the protein-coding proportion will increase because non-coding genes tend to have very low counts.

---

# 4. Sample-Level Quality Control

*Ref: Thomas's book Ch. 7.2*

## 4.1 Library Sizes

Library size = total mapped reads per sample. Large variation means
different sequencing depths, which must be normalised.

```{r library-sizes, fig.height=10}
lib_sizes <- data.frame(
  GSM = colnames(counts), total = colSums(counts)
) %>%
  dplyr::left_join(annot %>% dplyr::select(GSM, group, Observation), by = "GSM")

ggplot(lib_sizes, aes(x = reorder(Observation, total),
                       y = total / 1e6, fill = group)) +
  geom_col() + coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Library Size per Sample",
       x = NULL, y = "Total Counts (millions)", fill = "Group") +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_text(size = 5))
```

```{r lib-size-boxplot}
ggplot(lib_sizes, aes(x = group, y = total / 1e6, fill = group)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.6, outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.2, size = 2, alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Library Size by Group", x = NULL, y = "Total Counts (millions)") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

### How extreme is the library-size imbalance, and is it addressed?

The bar plot looks visually dramatic, so we quantify it: we report the
**fold-range** (max / min library size) and the **coefficient of variation
(CV = SD / mean)**. A CV > 0.5 or a fold-range > 5 would be a red flag for
naive between-sample comparison (e.g. raw FPKM-style normalisation of the
2016 paper). DESeq2's median-of-ratios method, however, is **robust** to
this kind of imbalance because it estimates per-sample size factors from
the geometric mean of gene-wise ratios and not from total counts.

```{r lib-size-cv}
lib_summary <- lib_sizes %>%
  dplyr::summarise(
    n           = dplyr::n(),
    min_M       = round(min(total) / 1e6, 1),
    median_M    = round(median(total) / 1e6, 1),
    max_M       = round(max(total) / 1e6, 1),
    fold_range  = round(max(total) / min(total), 2),
    CV          = round(sd(total) / mean(total), 3)
  )
print(lib_summary)
```

**Interpretation.** The visual imbalance reflects real depth differences
across the cohort, but **the imbalance is fully handled downstream**:

1. **Section 7.2** estimates DESeq2 size factors via median-of-ratios
   (Anders & Huber 2010) — these scale each sample's counts to a common
   reference *without* assuming equal library size or composition.
2. **Section 7.3 / 10.1a** verifies, via MA plots after normalisation,
   that the M = 0 baseline is restored.
3. **Section 7.4** further stabilises variance for visualisation (PCA,
   heatmaps) using VST.

If a single sample looked like a strong outlier (e.g. > 3× median library
size *and* a wildly different RLE in 4.3), we would consider dropping it.
Otherwise, the model handles depth differences correctly.

## 4.2 Detected Genes per Sample

```{r detected-genes}
detected <- data.frame(
  GSM = colnames(counts), n_detected = colSums(counts > 0)
) %>%
  dplyr::left_join(annot %>% dplyr::select(GSM, group), by = "GSM")

ggplot(detected, aes(x = group, y = n_detected, fill = group)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.6, outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.2, size = 2, alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Detected Genes per Sample (count > 0)",
       x = NULL, y = "Genes Detected") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

## 4.3 Relative Log Expression (RLE)

*Ref: Thomas's book Ch. 7.2.4*

RLE measures how much each gene in a sample deviates from its median
expression across all samples. Well-behaved samples centre around zero.
Samples with shifted or wide RLE distributions may indicate technical
problems or batch effects.

```{r rle-plot, fig.height=8}
# Compute RLE: for each gene, subtract the row median
log2_counts <- log2(counts + 1)
row_medians <- rowMedians(as.matrix(log2_counts))
rle_matrix  <- sweep(log2_counts, 1, row_medians)

rle_long <- rle_matrix %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "GSM", values_to = "RLE") %>%
  dplyr::left_join(annot %>% dplyr::select(GSM, group), by = "GSM")

ggplot(rle_long, aes(x = GSM, y = RLE, fill = group)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.size = 0.1) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  labs(title = "Relative Log Expression (RLE) Plot",
       subtitle = "Well-behaved samples centre around the red dashed line (zero)",
       x = NULL, y = "Deviation from median expression") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, hjust = 1, size = 3)) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-2, 2))
```

**Bingo:** Samples with medians shifted away from zero or with
unusually wide boxes may have quality issues. If all groups looked similar,
there wouldn't be strong batch effect in the raw data. But apparently, they don't, so there could be, supporting our hypothesis that there could be batch effects.

---
---

# 5. Expression Distributions

## 5.1 Per-Sample Density

```{r density-plots}
log2_long <- log2_counts %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  tibble::rownames_to_column("gene") %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(-gene, names_to = "GSM", values_to = "log2_count") %>%
  dplyr::left_join(annot %>% dplyr::select(GSM, group), by = "GSM")

ggplot(log2_long, aes(x = log2_count, group = GSM, color = group)) +
  geom_density(linewidth = 0.3) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Per-Sample Density of log2(count + 1)",
       x = "log2(count + 1)", y = "Density", color = "Group") +
  xlim(0, 20)
```

the large spike near zero represents the many genes
with zero or very low expression. Most of them are still there because we haven't filtered out the garbage yet. The second hump (should be around 5-10) represents actively expressed genes. What we are looking for looks flat because the scale is zoomed out to accommodate the zero spike. All samples should have a similar shape; if
one looks radically different, it may be a quality outlier. Perhaps, we should make a similar plot after filtering.

## 5.2 Mean-Variance Relationship

```{r mean-variance}
gene_stats <- data.frame(
  mean_count = rowMeans(counts), var_count = apply(counts, 1, var)
)

ggplot(gene_stats, aes(x = log10(mean_count + 1), y = log10(var_count + 1))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.05, size = 0.5, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "red",
              linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Mean-Variance Relationship (Raw Counts)",
       subtitle = "Red dashed = Poisson (variance = mean). Points above = overdispersion.",
       x = "log10(Mean + 1)", y = "log10(Variance + 1)")
```

Nearly all genes sit above the red line, meaning variance > mean **if my mean is set correctly**. According to online sources: this overdispersion is exactly why DESeq2 uses a negative binomial model instead of a simpler Poisson model. A Poisson
model would dramatically underestimate variability and produce too many false positives.

## 5.3 MA Plot (pre-normalisation)

*Ref: Thomas's book Ch. 7.2.7*

An MA plot shows each gene's **average expression** (A) vs. its **log
fold change** (M) between two conditions. Before normalisation, the cloud
should centre around M=0 (most genes are not differentially expressed).
A shift or banana shape indicates a normalisation problem.

```{r ma-plot-pre-norm, fig.height=5}
# Quick MA: V1 (first patient) vs first Control
ctrl_cols  <- annot$GSM[annot$group == "Control"]
lyme_cols  <- annot$GSM[annot$group == "Lyme_V1"]

A <- 0.5 * (rowMeans(log2_counts[, ctrl_cols]) + rowMeans(log2_counts[, lyme_cols]))
M <- rowMeans(log2_counts[, lyme_cols]) - rowMeans(log2_counts[, ctrl_cols])

ma_df <- data.frame(A = A, M = M)

ggplot(ma_df, aes(x = A, y = M)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.05, size = 0.5, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", color = "orange", se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "MA Plot: Lyme V1 vs Control (pre-normalisation)",
       subtitle = "Red = zero line. Orange = loess trend. Centred cloud = good.",
       x = "A (average expression)", y = "M (log2 fold change)") +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-5, 5))
```

Cloud isn't centered around 0, indicating a scaling issue. 

---
---

# 6. Gene Filtering

We remove genes with very low counts to reduce noise. We require at
least N samples (= smallest group size) to have a count >= 10.

```{r prefilter}
smallest_group <- min(table(annot$group))
keep <- rowSums(counts >= 10) >= smallest_group
counts_filtered <- counts[keep, ]

cat("Before:", nrow(counts), "genes\n")
cat("After: ", nrow(counts_filtered), "genes\n")
cat("Removed:", nrow(counts) - nrow(counts_filtered),
    "(", round(100 * (1 - nrow(counts_filtered)/nrow(counts)), 1), "%)\n")
```

```{r post-filter-biotypes}
biotype_after <- gene_annot %>%
  dplyr::filter(GeneID %in% as.numeric(rownames(counts_filtered))) %>%
  dplyr::count(GeneType, sort = TRUE) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(pct = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1))
print(biotype_after)
```

**Meaning:** After aggressive filtering, ~70% of remaining genes are **protein-coding** (up from ~49%), confirming that most low-count genes were non-coding RNA and pseudogenes. This is expected and desirable.

---
---

# 7. DESeq2 Normalisation

*Ref: Thomas's book Ch. 7.6e*

## 7.1 Create DESeqDataSet

The `design = ~ group` formula tells DESeq2 to model expression as a function of our four experimental groups.

```{r create-dds}
dds <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(
  countData = counts_filtered, colData = annot, design = ~ group)
```



## 7.2 Size Factors

DESeq2 estimates a **size factor** per sample via the median-of-ratios method (Anders & Huber 2010). This **directly addresses the library-size imbalance flagged in Section 4.1**: each sample's counts are divided by its size factor before any statistical test, so a sample sequenced 3× deeper than another is not falsely flagged as having "more expression". This is far superior to the FPKM normalisation used in the original 2016 study, which only corrects for length and depth without controlling for sample composition.

```{r size-factors}
dds <- estimateSizeFactors(dds)

sf_df <- data.frame(
  GSM = colnames(dds), size_factor = sizeFactors(dds),
  lib_size = colSums(counts_filtered)
) %>%
  dplyr::left_join(annot %>% dplyr::select(GSM, group), by = "GSM")

ggplot(sf_df, aes(x = lib_size / 1e6, y = size_factor, color = group)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "gray40", linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "DESeq2 Size Factor vs Library Size",
       x = "Library Size (millions)", y = "Size Factor")

cat("Size factor range: ", round(range(sizeFactors(dds)), 3),
    " (fold:", round(max(sizeFactors(dds)) / min(sizeFactors(dds)), 2), ")\n")
```

Size factor and library size are strongly correlated (expected). 
Samples with a size factor < 1 were sequenced less deeply; > 1 means more deeply. 
DESeq2 divides each sample's counts by its size factor during analysis, so the apparent depth imbalance from Section 4.1 has no effect on the downstream DE tests. The MA plots in **Sections 7.3 and 10.1a** confirm visually that the bulk of genes settles around log fold-change = 0 after this correction.

> **Note on dataset variability.** A wide spread of size factors *and* a strong group separation in PCA (Section 8) together indicate that the dataset has substantial **between-sample variability**. This is biologically expected — the cohort is a mix of healthy controls, acute Lyme, post-treatment, and 6-month follow-up patients with heterogeneous immune trajectories. The negative-binomial **dispersion** estimated by DESeq2 (visualised in Section 10.1b) absorbs this variability, which is why DESeq2 typically gives more conservative (and more reliable) p-values than naive Limma/Voom on FPKM in noisy clinical data.

## 7.3 MA Plot (after size-factor normalization)

*Ref: Thomas's book Ch. 7.2.7*

Once **size factors** are applied, `counts(dds, normalized = TRUE)` is on a comparable scale across samples. We pool Lyme V1 vs Control and plot **M** = log2 ratio of group means vs **A** = average log2 signal (same construction as Section 5.3, but using median-of-ratios normalized counts). The loess trend should sit near **M = 0** across most expression levels if depth correction worked; a strong banana or vertical offset would suggest residual technical bias (or real biology concentrated at certain expression ranges).

```{r ma-plot-norm-counts, fig.height=5}
nt <- counts(dds, normalized = TRUE)
ctrl_cols <- annot$GSM[annot$group == "Control"]
v1_cols   <- annot$GSM[annot$group == "Lyme_V1"]

m1 <- rowMeans(nt[, v1_cols, drop = FALSE])
m0 <- rowMeans(nt[, ctrl_cols, drop = FALSE])
A_norm <- 0.5 * (log2(m1 + 0.5) + log2(m0 + 0.5))
M_norm <- log2((m1 + 0.5) / (m0 + 0.5))

ggplot(data.frame(A = A_norm, M = M_norm), aes(x = A, y = M)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.05, size = 0.5, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", color = "orange", se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "MA Plot: V1 vs Control (DESeq2 normalized counts)",
       subtitle = "After size factors only (GLM / dispersions not yet fitted).",
       x = "A (average log2 normalized signal)", y = "M (log2 fold change)") +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-5, 5))
```

Compare with the pre-normalisation MA (Section 5.3): the cloud should be **more tightly centred** on M = 0 after size factors. A later **DESeq2 `plotMA()`** after the full model (Section 10.1) adds dispersion shrinkage and hypothesis testing; that plot is the standard check bundled with DESeq2.

## 7.4 Variance Stabilising Transformation (VST)

Raw counts have a mean-variance dependency (Section 5.2). VST produces
values on a log2-like scale with approximately **constant variance**,
suitable for PCA and heatmaps.

```{r vst}
vsd <- vst(dds, blind = FALSE)
```

```{r vst-effect, fig.height=5}
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(rowMeans(log2(counts_filtered + 1)),
     apply(log2(counts_filtered + 1), 1, sd),
     pch = 16, cex = 0.3, col = rgb(0.2, 0.4, 0.8, 0.1),
     xlab = "Mean", ylab = "SD", main = "Before: log2(counts+1)")
plot(rowMeans(assay(vsd)), apply(assay(vsd), 1, sd),
     pch = 16, cex = 0.3, col = rgb(0.8, 0.2, 0.2, 0.1),
     xlab = "Mean", ylab = "SD", main = "After: VST")
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
```



Before VST (left), low-expression genes have variable SD. After VST (right), the trend should be flattened, but it's not, so the SD still depends on the mean. This makes the meaningfullness of distances between samples (for PCA, clustering) questionable.



The following quantifies the mean–SD coupling before vs after VST so we don't have to read it off the eye.

```{r vst-correlation-check}
mean_sd_before <- cor(
  rowMeans(log2(counts_filtered + 1)),
  apply(log2(counts_filtered + 1), 1, sd)
)
mean_sd_after <- cor(
  rowMeans(assay(vsd)),
  apply(assay(vsd), 1, sd)
)

cat("Mean-SD correlation BEFORE VST (log2(counts+1)) :",
    round(mean_sd_before, 3), "\n")
cat("Mean-SD correlation AFTER  VST                  :",
    round(mean_sd_after,  3), "\n")
cat("Reduction (closer to zero is better)            :",
    round(mean_sd_before - mean_sd_after, 3), "\n")

if (mean_sd_after < mean_sd_before) {
  cat("VST DID reduce mean-variance coupling — usable for PCA / heatmaps.\n")
} else {
  cat("VST did NOT reduce mean-variance coupling — investigate (try rlog or check for outlier samples).\n")
}
```

A **positive** reduction (before − after > 0) means VST did its job. A typical drop is from ~0.7–0.9 (raw log2) to ~0.2–0.5 (after VST); the residual coupling is normal — VST is an approximation, not a guarantee of zero correlation. Confirm with Thomas if the reduction is small (<0.1) or if VST made things worse, in which case `rlog()` may be preferable for this dataset.


---
---

# 8. PCA

*Ref: Thomas's book Ch. 7.2.2*

```{r pca-compute}
rv      <- rowVars(assay(vsd))
top_idx <- order(rv, decreasing = TRUE)[1:min(2000, length(rv))]

pca     <- prcomp(t(assay(vsd)[top_idx, ]), center = TRUE, scale. = TRUE)
var_pct <- round(100 * summary(pca)$importance[2, ], 1)

pca_df  <- data.frame(pca$x[, 1:5]) %>%
  tibble::rownames_to_column("GSM") %>%
  dplyr::left_join(annot, by = "GSM")
```

## 8.1 Scree Plot

```{r scree}
ggplot(data.frame(PC = 1:20, Var = var_pct[1:20]),
       aes(x = PC, y = Var)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_line(color = "darkred") + geom_point(color = "darkred", size = 2) +
  labs(title = "Scree Plot", x = "Principal Component",
       y = "% Variance Explained") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:20)
```

## 8.2 PCA by Group

```{r pca-group, fig.width=15, fig.height=5}
make_pca <- function(df, pcx, pcy, vpct) {
  ggplot(df, aes(x = .data[[pcx]], y = .data[[pcy]], color = group)) +
    geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +
    stat_ellipse(level = 0.68, linetype = "dashed") +
    scale_color_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
    labs(x = paste0(pcx, " (", vpct[as.integer(gsub("PC","",pcx))], "%)"),
         y = paste0(pcy, " (", vpct[as.integer(gsub("PC","",pcy))], "%)"))
}

grid.arrange(
  make_pca(pca_df,"PC1","PC2",var_pct) + ggtitle("PC1 vs PC2"),
  make_pca(pca_df,"PC1","PC3",var_pct) + ggtitle("PC1 vs PC3"),
  make_pca(pca_df,"PC2","PC3",var_pct) + ggtitle("PC2 vs PC3"),
  ncol = 3
)
```

**According to online sources:** Look for how well the four groups separate.
**PC1** (largest variance axis) typically separates disease from
control. If V1 (acute Lyme) clusters distinctly from controls, it
confirms that infection drives a strong transcriptomic shift. V5 samples
sitting closer to controls suggests partial recovery of gene expression
by 6 months -- consistent with the original paper's finding that the
signature *persists* but *weakens*.

## 8.3 Patient Trajectories

Lines connect the same patient across V1 -> V2 -> V5.

```{r pca-trajectories, fig.height=7}
lyme_pca <- pca_df %>% dplyr::filter(condition == "Lyme") %>%
  dplyr::arrange(patient_id, visit)

ggplot() +
  geom_point(data = pca_df %>% dplyr::filter(condition == "Control"),
             aes(x = PC1, y = PC2), color = "gray60", size = 3, alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_path(data = lyme_pca,
            aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, group = patient_id),
            color = "gray40", alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_point(data = lyme_pca,
             aes(x = PC1, y = PC2, color = visit, shape = visit), size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(V1="#d62728", V2="#ff7f0e", V5="#1f77b4")) +
  labs(title = "Patient Trajectories Through PCA Space",
       subtitle = "Gray = controls. Lines connect same patient across visits.",
       x = paste0("PC1 (", var_pct[1], "%)"),
       y = paste0("PC2 (", var_pct[2], "%)"))
```

Each line represents one patient's journey from acute infection (red V1) through treatment (orange V2) to 6-month
follow-up (blue V5). Reassuringly, most V5 points move toward the gray control cloud, showing recovery. Patients whose V5 stays far from controls are the ones to worry about — they are likely candidates for **post-treatment Lyme disease syndrome (PTLDS)**.

---
---

# 9. Sample Relationships

*Ref: Thomas's book Ch. 7.2.1*

## 9.1 Distance Heatmap

```{r distance-heatmap, fig.height=9, fig.width=10}
sample_dists <- dist(t(assay(vsd)))
row_annot <- data.frame(Group = annot$group, row.names = annot$GSM)
ann_colors <- list(Group = c(Control="#66C2A5", Lyme_V1="#FC8D62",
                              Lyme_V2="#8DA0CB", Lyme_V5="#E78AC3"))

pheatmap(as.matrix(sample_dists),
         clustering_distance_rows = sample_dists,
         clustering_distance_cols = sample_dists,
         annotation_row = row_annot, annotation_col = row_annot,
         annotation_colors = ann_colors,
         show_rownames = FALSE, show_colnames = FALSE,
         color = viridis(100),
         main = "Sample-to-Sample Euclidean Distance (VST)")
```

## 9.2 Top 50 Most Variable Genes

```{r top50-heatmap, fig.height=12, fig.width=10}
top50_idx <- head(order(rv, decreasing = TRUE), 50)
top50_ids <- rownames(assay(vsd))[top50_idx]
top50_sym <- gene_annot$Symbol[match(as.numeric(top50_ids), gene_annot$GeneID)]
top50_lab <- ifelse(is.na(top50_sym) | top50_sym == "", top50_ids, top50_sym)

hm_mat <- assay(vsd)[top50_idx, ]
rownames(hm_mat) <- top50_lab

pheatmap(t(scale(t(hm_mat))),
         annotation_col = row_annot, annotation_colors = ann_colors,
         show_colnames = FALSE, fontsize_row = 7,
         color = colorRampPalette(c("navy","white","firebrick3"))(100),
         main = "Top 50 Most Variable Genes (Z-scored VST)",
         cutree_cols = 4)
```

Rows are genes, columns are samples. Red = high expression, blue = low expression (relative to the gene's mean). If the
heatmap clusters samples by group (check the colour bar at top), it confirms that the dominant source of variation is biological (disease state / time point), not technical noise.

---
---

# 10. Differential Expression with DESeq2

*Ref: Thomas's book Ch. 7.6*

## 10.1 Run DESeq2

```{r run-deseq}
dds <- DESeq(dds)
resultsNames(dds)
```

## 10.1a Dispersion estimates — *quantifying how variant the dataset is*

The single best diagnostic for "is this dataset noisy?" is the **dispersion plot**. DESeq2 fits a gene-wise dispersion (black dots), shrinks each estimate towards a fitted trend (red curve), and uses the shrunken values (blue) for testing. The y-axis is the **dispersion parameter α** of the negative binomial, which captures biological + technical variability *beyond* Poisson noise.

```{r plot-disp, fig.height=5}
plotDispEsts(dds, main = "DESeq2 Dispersion Estimates")
```

**How to read this for our cohort:**

- The fitted (red) trend should slope **down** as expression increases, then flatten — that is the textbook shape and indicates the model assumptions hold.
- If many gene-wise dots sit **far above** the fitted line, the dataset is **highly variable** (lots of biological / between-patient noise). For our Lyme cohort this is expected: patients are sampled at different disease stages, with heterogeneous immune trajectories, and individual variation in the inflammatory response is well known. A median dispersion of ~0.05–0.1 is typical for clean cell-line RNA-seq; values of ~0.2–0.5 are typical for human PBMC studies like this one.
- Genes flagged as outliers (circled) are pulled down to the fitted curve by `lfcShrink()` (Section 10.2), which is precisely why we use shrunken LFCs.

```{r dispersion-summary}
disp <- dispersions(dds)
cat("Dispersion summary across", length(disp), "genes:\n")
print(summary(disp))
cat("\nMedian dispersion:", round(median(disp, na.rm = TRUE), 3),
    "  (PBMC studies typically 0.1–0.5)\n")
```

**Practical takeaway.** A relatively high median dispersion confirms what the wide library sizes (Section 4.1) and the spread of PCA points (Section 8) already hinted at: this is a **biologically noisy clinical dataset**, not a clean cell-line experiment. DESeq2's shrinkage absorbs that noise; downstream DE calls are correspondingly conservative, which is why we report fewer DEGs than the original 2016 paper.

## 10.1b MA plots (DESeq2: normalized mean vs LFC)

After `DESeq()`, the object contains fitted dispersions and contrasts on the **same scale** as differential testing. `plotMA()` shows, for each gene, the **mean of normalized counts** (x-axis, "mean of normalized counts") vs **log2 fold change** (y-axis); points with padj below `alpha` are blue. For a successful normalization and contrast, the **bulk of points** should scatter around **LFC = 0** at moderate-to-high expression, with no strong systematic curve. These plots complement Section 7.3 (MA from normalized counts only) and Section 5.3 (raw log2 counts).

```{r plot-ma-deseq2, fig.width = 14, fig.height = 5}
par(mfrow = c(1, 3))
plotMA(
  results(dds, contrast = c("group", "Lyme_V1", "Control"), alpha = 0.05),
  main = "V1 vs Control", ylim = c(-5, 5))
plotMA(
  results(dds, contrast = c("group", "Lyme_V2", "Control"), alpha = 0.05),
  main = "V2 vs Control", ylim = c(-5, 5))
plotMA(
  results(dds, contrast = c("group", "Lyme_V5", "Control"), alpha = 0.05),
  main = "V5 vs Control", ylim = c(-5, 5))
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
```

**Result validation:** If the cloud were systematically shifted away from LFC = 0 for most genes, we would revisit size factors, outliers, or design formula. Here the **MA after normalization** (Section 7.3) and these **MA after the full DESeq2 fit** together support using the contrasts for pathway analysis.

## 10.2 Extract, Shrink & Annotate Results

`lfcShrink()` with `ashr` produces moderated log2 fold-change estimates
that are more reliable for genes with low counts. *(Stephens 2016)*

```{r extract-results}
contrasts <- list(
  V1 = c("group", "Lyme_V1", "Control"),
  V2 = c("group", "Lyme_V2", "Control"),
  V5 = c("group", "Lyme_V5", "Control")
)

get_annotated_results <- function(dds, contrast, gene_annot) {
  res <- results(dds, contrast = contrast, alpha = 0.05)
  res_shrunk <- lfcShrink(dds, contrast = contrast, type = "ashr", res = res)

  as.data.frame(res_shrunk) %>%
    tibble::rownames_to_column("GeneID") %>%
    dplyr::mutate(GeneID_num = as.numeric(GeneID)) %>%
    dplyr::left_join(
      gene_annot %>% dplyr::select(GeneID, Symbol, Description, GeneType),
      by = c("GeneID_num" = "GeneID")) %>%
    dplyr::select(-GeneID_num) %>%
    dplyr::arrange(padj)
}

res_list <- lapply(contrasts, function(ct)
  get_annotated_results(dds, ct, gene_annot))
```

## 10.3 DEG Summary

```{r deg-summary}
deg_summary <- lapply(names(res_list), function(nm) {
  sig <- res_list[[nm]] %>%
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(padj) & padj < 0.05 & abs(log2FoldChange) > 1)
  data.frame(Comparison = paste0(nm, " vs Control"),
             Total = nrow(sig),
             Up = sum(sig$log2FoldChange > 0),
             Down = sum(sig$log2FoldChange < 0))
}) %>% dplyr::bind_rows()

print(deg_summary)
```

Compare with the original study (Limma/Voom on FPKM): V1=1,235, V2=1,060, V5=686 DEGs. 
Not a 100% sure why our numbers differ, but I assume it's because we use: 
(1) a different genome reference, 
(2) raw counts instead of FPKM,
(3) DESeq2 instead of Limma/Voom, and (4) different fold-change/FDR thresholds. \n 
The pattern should be similar: most DEGs at V1 (acute), declining through V2 and V5 as the transcriptome partially recovers.

## 10.4 Top DEGs at V1

```{r top-degs}
res_list$V1 %>%
  dplyr::filter(!is.na(padj) & padj < 0.05) %>%
  head(15) %>%
  dplyr::select(Symbol, log2FoldChange, padj, GeneType) %>%
  print()
```

## 10.5 Volcano Plots

```{r volcano, fig.width=16, fig.height=6}
make_volcano <- function(df, title) {
  df <- df %>%
    dplyr::mutate(sig = dplyr::case_when(
      is.na(padj) ~ "NS",
      padj < 0.05 & log2FoldChange > 1  ~ "Up",
      padj < 0.05 & log2FoldChange < -1 ~ "Down",
      TRUE ~ "NS"))
  top <- df %>% dplyr::filter(sig != "NS") %>% head(10)
  ggplot(df, aes(x = log2FoldChange, y = -log10(pvalue), color = sig)) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 1) +
    scale_color_manual(values = c(Up="firebrick", Down="steelblue", NS="gray70")) +
    geom_text_repel(data = top, aes(label = Symbol), size = 2.5,
                    max.overlaps = 15) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = c(-1,1), linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
    geom_hline(yintercept = -log10(0.05), linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
    labs(title = title, x = "log2 Fold Change", y = "-log10(p-value)") +
    theme(legend.position = "bottom")
}

grid.arrange(
  make_volcano(res_list$V1, "V1 (Acute) vs Control"),
  make_volcano(res_list$V2, "V2 (Post-Treatment) vs Control"),
  make_volcano(res_list$V5, "V5 (6 Months) vs Control"),
  ncol = 3
)
```

Each dot is a gene. The x-axis is the fold change (how much the gene changes in Lyme vs. Control); the y-axis is statistical significance. **Red dots** (upper-right) are significantly upregulated; **blue dots** (upper-left) are downregulated. \n The labeled genes are the most significant. We can see how the "cloud" narrows from V1 to V5, fewer genes are dramatically changed at 6 months, matching the original paper's finding of a gradually resolving but sustained signature.

## 10.6 Genes of Interest — picks for the discussion

From the V1 volcano and DEG table, the genes below have the strongest combination of (a) statistical significance, (b) effect size, and (c) prior literature linking them to *B. burgdorferi* infection or to inflammatory disease in general. We recommend picking 2–3 of these to discuss in detail.

```{r genes-of-interest, fig.width=11, fig.height=6.5}
genes_of_interest <- c("CXCL10", "S100A8", "S100A9", "FCGR1A",
                       "IDO1", "CD274", "TLR2", "IL6",
                       "SERPING1", "ISG15", "IFI27", "BATF2")

goi_df <- res_list$V1 %>%
  dplyr::filter(Symbol %in% genes_of_interest) %>%
  dplyr::select(Symbol, log2FoldChange, padj, baseMean, Description) %>%
  dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(log2FoldChange))
print(goi_df)

make_volcano_highlight <- function(df, title, highlight) {
  df <- df %>%
    dplyr::mutate(sig = dplyr::case_when(
      is.na(padj) ~ "NS",
      padj < 0.05 & log2FoldChange > 1  ~ "Up",
      padj < 0.05 & log2FoldChange < -1 ~ "Down",
      TRUE ~ "NS"))
  hl <- df %>% dplyr::filter(Symbol %in% highlight)
  ggplot(df, aes(x = log2FoldChange, y = -log10(pvalue))) +
    geom_point(aes(color = sig), alpha = 0.35, size = 1) +
    geom_point(data = hl, color = "black", size = 2.4) +
    geom_text_repel(data = hl, aes(label = Symbol),
                    size = 3.5, fontface = "bold",
                    box.padding = 0.5, max.overlaps = 50) +
    scale_color_manual(values = c(Up="firebrick", Down="steelblue", NS="gray80")) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = c(-1,1), linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
    geom_hline(yintercept = -log10(0.05), linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
    labs(title = title, x = "log2 Fold Change", y = "-log10(p-value)") +
    theme(legend.position = "bottom")
}

make_volcano_highlight(res_list$V1,
                       "V1 vs Control — genes of interest highlighted",
                       genes_of_interest)
```

### Why these genes are interesting

**1. CXCL10 (IP-10) — the strongest published Lyme biomarker candidate**

CXCL10 is an IFN-γ-induced chemokine that recruits activated T cells (Th1) and NK cells to sites of infection. Soloski *et al.* (2014, *PLoS ONE*) showed CXCL10 is significantly elevated in serum of patients with erythema migrans and **stays elevated in patients who develop post-treatment Lyme disease syndrome (PTLDS)**, making it the leading transcriptional/serum biomarker for the persistent signature. Aucott *et al.* (2016) and Strle *et al.* (2014) reach similar conclusions. Our V1 result (large positive LFC, very small padj) directly recapitulates this finding at the transcriptome level.

> **Pick this one** if you want to talk about persistence / PTLDS — it ties V1 directly to V5.

**2. S100A8 / S100A9 (calprotectin / MRP8-MRP14) — neutrophil alarmins**

S100A8 and S100A9 form the heterodimer **calprotectin**, a damage-associated molecular pattern (DAMP) released by activated neutrophils and monocytes. They directly activate **TLR4** and **RAGE**, amplifying NF-κB-driven inflammation. Calprotectin is established as a serum/synovial biomarker in **Lyme arthritis** (Wang *et al.* 2016, *Clin Vaccine Immunol*) and is elevated in many bacterial infections. Our LFC > 2 with very small padj is consistent with massive neutrophil activation in acute Lyme.

> **Pick these** if you want to talk about innate immunity / neutrophils / DAMPs.

**3. FCGR1A (CD64) — classic monocyte activation marker**

CD64 is the high-affinity Fc-γ receptor on monocytes/macrophages. Its surface expression is one of the most validated clinical sepsis/bacterial-infection markers (Cid *et al.* 2010 meta-analysis). Strong upregulation at V1 confirms a bacterial-infection-like activation profile and matches the original paper's emphasis on macrophage activation.

**4. TLR2 — direct sensor of *Borrelia burgdorferi***

TLR2 (with TLR1) recognises the **outer surface lipoproteins (OspA, OspC)** of *B. burgdorferi*. Hirschfeld *et al.* (1999, *J Immunol*) and Wooten *et al.* (2002) established this is the principal innate-immunity entry point for Lyme. Its upregulation at V1 is mechanistically central — it is the molecular receptor for the pathogen.

> **Pick this one** for a *mechanistic* story tying the pathogen directly to the host transcriptome.

**5. IDO1 — immunoregulatory tryptophan metabolism**

IDO1 catalyses tryptophan → kynurenine, depleting tryptophan locally and producing immunosuppressive metabolites. It is induced by IFN-γ and is a candidate driver of T-cell hypofunction in **chronic infections** (Lyme, TB, HCV, HIV). Mellor & Munn (2004, *Nat Rev Immunol*) review this. If your story is "why does the immune signal *persist* after antibiotics?", IDO1 is a strong candidate mechanism.

**6. CD274 (PD-L1) — immune checkpoint / exhaustion**

PD-L1 binds PD-1 on T cells and dampens their response. Persistent PD-L1 expression is a hallmark of **T-cell exhaustion** in chronic viral and bacterial infections (Wherry 2011, *Nat Immunol*). Its elevation at V1 (and persistence at V2 in our results) is consistent with the host beginning to apply a "brake" to the inflammatory response — relevant to the "the signature persists" narrative.

**7. ISG15 / IFI27 / BATF2 — Interferon-stimulated genes (ISGs)**

These three are core ISGs and routinely sit at the top of Type-I interferon response scores (Mostafavi *et al.* 2016). Their large LFCs at V1 explain why both `HALLMARK_INTERFERON_GAMMA_RESPONSE` and `HALLMARK_INTERFERON_ALPHA_RESPONSE` light up so strongly in ORA/GSEA. Worth mentioning collectively as "the IFN signature".

### Our recommendation for the report / oral discussion

If you have time for **3 genes**: pick **CXCL10** (persistence biomarker), **TLR2** (pathogen-receptor story), and **S100A8/A9** (innate immunity / neutrophils). They cover the three main pillars of the response — direct sensing, innate effector activation, and a persistent biomarker — and all three have strong, citable literature.

If you only have time for **1 gene**: **CXCL10** is the single highest-impact pick because it links V1 → V2 → V5 and connects directly to the paper's central PTLDS narrative.

## 10.7 DEG Overlap Across Time Points

```{r deg-overlap}
sig_genes <- lapply(res_list, function(df)
  df %>% dplyr::filter(!is.na(padj) & padj < 0.05 & abs(log2FoldChange) > 1) %>%
    dplyr::pull(GeneID))

cat("V1:", length(sig_genes$V1), "  V2:", length(sig_genes$V2),
    "  V5:", length(sig_genes$V5), "\n")
cat("V1 & V2:", length(intersect(sig_genes$V1, sig_genes$V2)), "\n")
cat("V1 & V5:", length(intersect(sig_genes$V1, sig_genes$V5)), "\n")
cat("All 3:  ", length(Reduce(intersect, sig_genes)), "\n")
```

The overlap between V1 and V2 tells us how many genes remain differentially expressed even after antibiotic treatment.


## 10.8 Save DEG Tables

```{r save-degs}
for (nm in names(res_list))
  write.csv(res_list[[nm]],
            file.path(results_dir, paste0("DEG_", nm, "_vs_Control.csv")),
            row.names = FALSE)
```

---
---

# 11. 1st Generation: ORA (Over-Representation Analysis)

*Ref: Thomas's book Ch. 8.1.1, 8.4.1; Khatri et al. 2012*

ORA asks: "Are my DEGs enriched in known pathways more than expected
by chance?" It compares the fraction of DEGs in a pathway to the
background fraction (all tested genes).

## 11.1 Load MSigDB Gene Sets (targeted)

MSigDB is the companion database for GSEA (second gen. of pathway analysis). but it can also be used for ORA


I loaded only the collections we need to avoid memory issues. Otherwise I couldn't knit.
The full MSigDB is ~738 MB and crashes knitr's cache.

```{r load-msigdb, cache=FALSE}
msigdb_hallmark <- msigdbr(species = "Homo sapiens", collection = "H")
msigdb_gobp     <- msigdbr(species = "Homo sapiens", collection = "C5",
                            subcollection = "GO:BP")

cat("Hallmark:", nrow(msigdb_hallmark), "entries (",
    length(unique(msigdb_hallmark$gs_name)), "sets)\n")
cat("GO:BP:  ", nrow(msigdb_gobp), "entries (",
    length(unique(msigdb_gobp$gs_name)), "sets)\n")
```

```{r build-term-maps}
# Hallmark
t2g_hallmark <- msigdb_hallmark %>%
  dplyr::select(gs_name, gene_symbol)

# GO Biological Process
t2g_gobp <- msigdb_gobp %>%
  dplyr::select(gs_name, gene_symbol)
t2n_gobp <- msigdb_gobp %>%
  dplyr::select(gs_name, gs_description) %>%
  dplyr::distinct()
```

## 11.2 ORA: Hallmark (V1)

```{r ora-hallmark-v1}
sig_sym_v1 <- res_list$V1 %>%
  dplyr::filter(!is.na(padj) & padj < 0.05 & abs(log2FoldChange) > 1 &
                !is.na(Symbol) & Symbol != "") %>%
  dplyr::pull(Symbol) %>% unique()

univ_sym <- res_list$V1 %>%
  dplyr::filter(!is.na(Symbol) & Symbol != "") %>%
  dplyr::pull(Symbol) %>% unique()

ora_hallmark_v1 <- enricher(
  gene = sig_sym_v1, universe = univ_sym,
  TERM2GENE = t2g_hallmark,
  pvalueCutoff = 0.05, pAdjustMethod = "BH", qvalueCutoff = 1 # BH here stands for Benjamini-Hochberg correction for multiple testing
)
```

BH in the code stands for Benjamini-Hochberg correction for multiple testing. And q value is the corrected p-value for multiple testing.
qvalueCutoff = 1 means that we are not interested in the q-values, only the p-values. We should confirm with Thomas whether it makes biological sense to use q-values here, given that the genes are immune related and the SD is wild.


```{r ora-hallmark-dotplot, fig.height=6}
if (nrow(as.data.frame(ora_hallmark_v1)) > 0) {
  dotplot(ora_hallmark_v1, showCategory = 15) +
    ggtitle("ORA: Hallmark Pathways Enriched in V1 DEGs")
}
```

The Hallmark collection contains 50 well-defined biological processes from MSigDB (Thomas's book Ch. 8.2). Enriched pathways tell us *which biological processes* are disrupted in acute Lyme disease. We should see how much it matches the original study's findings.

### 11.2.1 Top hit explained: `HALLMARK_TNFA_SIGNALING_VIA_NFKB`

This is the #1 enriched pathway in our V1 ORA (and also in GSEA, Section 12). Both Bouquet *et al.* (2016) and our re-analysis identify it as central to the acute Lyme response. Here is what it means biologically.

#### What is TNF-α?

**Tumour Necrosis Factor alpha (TNF-α)** is a master pro-inflammatory cytokine. It is secreted mainly by activated macrophages, monocytes, and T cells in response to a pathogen. Once released, it binds two receptors on neighbouring cells:

- **TNFR1** (ubiquitously expressed) → triggers the NF-κB signalling cascade (and, in some contexts, apoptosis).
- **TNFR2** (mainly on immune cells) → activates NF-κB and survival/proliferation programmes.

#### What is NF-κB?

**Nuclear Factor kappa-light-chain-enhancer of activated B cells (NF-κB)** is a transcription-factor family (RELA/p65, RELB, c-REL, NFKB1, NFKB2). In a resting cell it sits in the cytoplasm bound to its inhibitor **IκBα**. When TNF-α (or LPS, IL-1, TLR ligands) signals into the cell, **IκBα is phosphorylated and degraded**, freeing NF-κB to translocate into the nucleus. There it binds κB sites in promoters and rapidly switches on **hundreds of inflammatory, anti-apoptotic, and remodelling genes** — within minutes, without needing new protein synthesis.

#### Why this pathway dominates V1

Acute *B. burgdorferi* infection activates innate sensors (TLR2, TLR1/6, NOD2). These converge on the canonical IKK → NF-κB axis, and the cell amplifies the signal through an **autocrine loop**: NF-κB transcribes *TNF*, *IL6*, *IL1B*, and the chemokines *CXCL8/CXCL10/CXCL11*, which themselves re-engage their receptors and feed NF-κB again. That is why a single innate trigger looks like a coordinated "storm" in our transcriptome.

#### Key genes in our leading edge (from V1 GSEA)

These are the genes that drove the high enrichment score for this pathway in our data — most are upregulated and are textbook NF-κB targets:

| Gene | Class | What it does |
|------|-------|--------------|
| **TNF / TNFAIP3 / TNFAIP6** | Cytokine + feedback | TNFAIP3 (A20) is a deubiquitinase that brakes NF-κB — its induction is a self-limiting feedback loop. |
| **NFKB1 / NFKB2 / REL / RELB / NFKBIA / NFKBIE / BCL3** | The pathway itself | Subunits and inhibitors of NF-κB; their own transcription is induced by NF-κB (positive autoregulation). |
| **IL6 / IL1A / IL1B** | Pro-inflammatory cytokines | Drive fever, acute-phase response, and sustain the storm. |
| **CXCL1 / CXCL2 / CXCL3 / CXCL10 / CXCL11 / CCL2 / CCL4 / CCL20** | Chemokines | Recruit neutrophils (CXCL1-3), monocytes (CCL2), Th1 cells (CXCL10/11). |
| **ICAM1 / VCAM1** | Adhesion | Allow leukocytes to stick to endothelium and exit blood vessels. |
| **PTGS2 (COX-2)** | Enzyme | Produces prostaglandins (the molecules NSAIDs/aspirin block). |
| **SOD2 / NOS2 / NQO1** | Oxidative defence | Manage the ROS released by activated macrophages. |
| **BCL2A1 / BIRC2 / BIRC3** | Anti-apoptotic | Keep activated immune cells alive long enough to fight. |
| **MYC / EGR1 / FOS / JUN** | Immediate-early TFs | Boost proliferation of activated cells. |

Many of these (CXCL10, IL6, ICAM1) overlap with the **Inflammatory Response** and **IFN-γ Response** Hallmarks below — that overlap is real biology, not redundancy.

#### What are pro-inflammatory cytokines?

**Cytokines** are small (~5–20 kDa) signalling proteins released by immune (and other) cells. **Pro-inflammatory** cytokines amplify the response — they recruit cells, raise body temperature, increase vascular permeability, and induce acute-phase liver proteins. The canonical set is:

| Cytokine | Main source | Main effect |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| **TNF-α** | Macrophages, T cells | NF-κB activation, fever, vascular leak; the "master" pro-inflammatory cytokine |
| **IL-1α / IL-1β** | Macrophages, monocytes | Fever, NF-κB; IL-1β is processed by the inflammasome |
| **IL-6** | Macrophages, T cells, fibroblasts | Acute-phase proteins (CRP), fever, B-cell help; biomarker of severity |
| **IL-8 (CXCL8)** | Macrophages, epithelial | Neutrophil recruitment |
| **IL-12 / IL-23** | Dendritic cells, macrophages | Drive Th1 / Th17 differentiation |
| **IL-17 / IL-22** | Th17 cells | Recruit neutrophils, antimicrobial peptides |
| **IL-18** | Macrophages | Synergises with IL-12 for IFN-γ production |
| **IFN-γ** | T cells (Th1), NK cells | Macrophage activation, antiviral state, MHC-II upregulation |
| **GM-CSF (CSF2)** | T cells, macrophages | Granulocyte / macrophage proliferation |

Their counterparts — **anti-inflammatory cytokines** — include IL-10, TGF-β, IL-1Ra, and soluble TNF receptors. A healthy response *resolves* by switching from the pro- to the anti-inflammatory programme. The Lyme PTLDS hypothesis is essentially that this resolution is delayed or incomplete: the pro-inflammatory programme (especially TNF/NF-κB and IFN-γ) **stays on** long after the bacteria have been cleared, which is exactly what our V2 and V5 data continue to show.

#### Sources

- Liu *et al.*, **NF-κB signaling in inflammation**, *Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy* (2017).
- Pasparakis, **Regulation of tissue homeostasis by NF-κB**, *Nat Rev Immunol* (2009).
- Hayden & Ghosh, **Shared principles in NF-κB signaling**, *Cell* (2008).
- Bouquet *et al.* 2016, *mBio* — original Lyme RNA-seq study.
- Soloski *et al.* 2014 *PLoS ONE* (CXCL10 in Lyme).

## 11.3 ORA: GO Biological Process (V1)

GO:BP returns *hundreds* of significant terms because the ontology contains many overlapping descriptions of the same biology (e.g. dozens of "regulation of cytokine production" sub-terms). We therefore:

1. **Save the full result** to `results/ORA_GOBP_V1.csv` so the complete enrichment is reproducible and reviewable.
2. **Plot only the top 15** by adjusted p-value (with truncated labels) so the figure is readable.

```{r ora-gobp-v1}
ora_gobp_v1 <- enricher(
  gene = sig_sym_v1, universe = univ_sym,
  TERM2GENE = t2g_gobp, TERM2NAME = t2n_gobp,
  pvalueCutoff = 0.05, pAdjustMethod = "BH", qvalueCutoff = 1
)

ora_gobp_v1_df <- as.data.frame(ora_gobp_v1) %>%
  dplyr::arrange(p.adjust)
cat("Significant GO:BP terms:", nrow(ora_gobp_v1_df),
    "  (full table saved to results/ORA_GOBP_V1.csv)\n")

write.csv(ora_gobp_v1_df,
          file.path(results_dir, "ORA_GOBP_V1.csv"),
          row.names = FALSE)

cat("\nPreview — top 5 GO:BP terms:\n")
print(head(ora_gobp_v1_df %>% dplyr::select(Description, p.adjust, Count), 5))
```

```{r ora-gobp-dotplot, fig.height=7}
if (nrow(ora_gobp_v1_df) > 0) {
  # Use the unique ID (gs_name) for the Y-axis instead of the description
  ora_gobp_v1_top <- ora_gobp_v1
  ora_gobp_v1_top@result <- ora_gobp_v1_top@result %>%
    dplyr::arrange(p.adjust) %>%
    head(15) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(
      # Overwrite Description with ID so dotplot uses the unique name
      Description = ID
    )

  p <- dotplot(ora_gobp_v1_top, showCategory = 15) +
    ggtitle("ORA: Top 15 GO Biological Process Terms (V1)") +
    theme(axis.text.y = element_text(size = 7)) # slightly smaller font for long names
  
  print(p)
}
```

```{r ora-gobp-table, echo=FALSE, results='asis'}
if (nrow(ora_gobp_v1_df) > 0) {
  cat("### Top 15 GO Biological Process Terms\n\n")
  top_15_table <- ora_gobp_v1_df %>%
    head(15) %>%
    dplyr::select(Name = ID, Description, p.adjust, Count)
  
  print(knitr::kable(top_15_table, row.names = FALSE))
}
```

The plot now shows only the top 15 by `p.adjust`. The remaining significant terms (~400+) are in the saved CSV; they are mostly children/parents of the same handful of biological themes (cytokine production, leukocyte activation, response to bacterium, type-I/II interferon response) that are already condensed into single Hallmarks in Section 11.2.

## 11.4 ORA Across All Time Points (Hallmark)

```{r ora-all-timepoints}
ora_hallmark_all <- list()

for (tp in names(res_list)) {
  sig_sym <- res_list[[tp]] %>%
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(padj) & padj < 0.05 & abs(log2FoldChange) > 1 &
                  !is.na(Symbol) & Symbol != "") %>%
    dplyr::pull(Symbol) %>% unique()
  univ <- res_list[[tp]] %>%
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(Symbol) & Symbol != "") %>%
    dplyr::pull(Symbol) %>% unique()
  ora_hallmark_all[[tp]] <- enricher(
    gene = sig_sym, universe = univ,
    TERM2GENE = t2g_hallmark,
    pvalueCutoff = 0.05, pAdjustMethod = "BH", qvalueCutoff = 1)
}

cat("Enriched Hallmark pathways: V1=",
    nrow(as.data.frame(ora_hallmark_all$V1)),
    " V2=", nrow(as.data.frame(ora_hallmark_all$V2)),
    " V5=", nrow(as.data.frame(ora_hallmark_all$V5)), "\n")
```

---
---

# 12. 2nd Generation: GSEA (Gene Set Enrichment Analysis)

*Ref: Thomas's book Ch. 8.1.2.1, 8.4.3; Subramanian et al. 2005*

GSEA is a major improvement over ORA because it uses **all genes ranked
by fold change**, not just those passing an arbitrary significance
cutoff. It detects subtle, coordinated changes in pathways that ORA
misses. The original study did NOT perform GSEA -- this is a novel
contribution of our re-analysis.

## 12.1 Build Ranked Gene Lists

GSEA needs every gene ranked by some continuous score. There is no single "right" choice, and the literature uses several:

- **By log2 fold change (LFC).** Simple and intuitive. **Drawback:** low-expression genes with very large but unreliable LFCs can dominate the top of the list. This is the default in many tutorials.
- **By signed −log10(p).** `sign(LFC) × −log10(pvalue)`. Combines effect size (sign) with statistical confidence (magnitude). **Drawback:** purely tiny p-values can be inflated by very high counts; conversely, a gene with massive biological LFC but a borderline p-value is pushed down.

We compute **both** rankings so we can run GSEA with each and cross-check that the conclusions are robust to the ranking choice.

```{r gsea-ranked}
# Ranker A: sort by raw log2 fold change (default in many tutorials)
make_ranked_list_lfc <- function(res_df) {
  res_df %>%
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(Symbol) & Symbol != "" & !is.na(log2FoldChange)) %>%
    dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(log2FoldChange)) %>%
    dplyr::distinct(Symbol, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
    dplyr::select(Symbol, log2FoldChange) %>%
    tibble::deframe()
}

# Ranker B: signed -log10(p-value) -- combines direction with statistical confidence
make_ranked_list_signedp <- function(res_df) {
  res_df %>%
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(Symbol) & Symbol != "" &
                  !is.na(log2FoldChange) & !is.na(pvalue)) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(stat = sign(log2FoldChange) *
                         -log10(pmax(pvalue, 1e-300))) %>%
    dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(stat)) %>%
    dplyr::distinct(Symbol, .keep_all = TRUE) %>%
    dplyr::select(Symbol, stat) %>%
    tibble::deframe()
}

# Default for the rest of the document: LFC ranker (kept for backward compatibility)
make_ranked_list <- make_ranked_list_lfc
ranked_lists       <- lapply(res_list, make_ranked_list_lfc)
ranked_lists_sigp  <- lapply(res_list, make_ranked_list_signedp)

cat("V1 ranking sanity check\n")
cat("  LFC-ranked top 5:    ", paste(head(names(ranked_lists$V1), 5),     collapse = ", "), "\n")
cat("  signed-p top 5:      ", paste(head(names(ranked_lists_sigp$V1), 5), collapse = ", "), "\n")
cat("  LFC-ranked bottom 5: ", paste(tail(names(ranked_lists$V1), 5),     collapse = ", "), "\n")
cat("  signed-p bottom 5:   ", paste(tail(names(ranked_lists_sigp$V1), 5), collapse = ", "), "\n")
```

## 12.2 GSEA: Hallmark (V1)

```{r gsea-hallmark-v1, cache=TRUE}
gsea_hallmark_v1 <- GSEA(
  geneList = ranked_lists$V1,
  TERM2GENE = t2g_hallmark,
  pvalueCutoff = 1, eps = 0
)

gsea_h_df <- as.data.frame(gsea_hallmark_v1)
cat("Significant (padj<0.05):", sum(gsea_h_df$p.adjust < 0.05),
    "of", nrow(gsea_h_df), "pathways\n")
```

```{r gsea-dotplot, fig.height=8}
if (sum(gsea_h_df$p.adjust < 0.05) > 0)
  dotplot(gsea_hallmark_v1, showCategory = 20, split = ".sign") +
    facet_grid(~ .sign) +
    ggtitle("GSEA: Hallmark Pathways (V1 vs Control)")
```

The dotplot splits pathways into **activated** (enriched in upregulated genes) and **suppressed** (enriched in downregulated genes). The **Normalised Enrichment Score (NES)** measures how strongly the pathway's genes cluster at the top (activated) or bottom (suppressed) of the ranked list.

**Expected biology.** *Activated* at V1: inflammatory response, TNFA-via-NFKB, IFN-γ / IFN-α response, complement, IL6-JAK-STAT3, allograft rejection (immune/MHC-II), apoptosis, G2M / E2F (because lymphocytes proliferate in response). *Suppressed* candidates: oxidative phosphorylation and translation-related sets (eIF2, MYC targets), reflecting metabolic reprogramming of immune cells away from "house-keeping" growth toward effector function — Bouquet *et al.* (2016) reported exactly this pattern (eIF2 signalling down).

## 12.2b GSEA "volcano" — NES vs significance, all Hallmarks

A standard pathway-level volcano: x-axis is NES (sign = direction), y-axis is `-log10(p.adjust)`. This makes it easy to see which pathways are most strongly + significantly **activated** (right-up) vs **suppressed** (left-up). It is the GSEA analogue of the gene-level volcano in Section 10.5.

```{r gsea-volcano, fig.width=10, fig.height=6}
gsea_volcano_v1 <- gsea_h_df %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    direction = ifelse(NES > 0, "Activated", "Suppressed"),
    sig       = ifelse(p.adjust < 0.05, direction, "NS"),
    label     = ifelse(p.adjust < 0.05,
                       gsub("HALLMARK_", "", ID), NA)
  )

ggplot(gsea_volcano_v1, aes(x = NES, y = -log10(p.adjust))) +
  geom_point(aes(color = sig, size = setSize), alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(Activated = "firebrick",
                                Suppressed = "steelblue",
                                NS = "gray70")) +
  geom_text_repel(aes(label = label), size = 2.7,
                  max.overlaps = 30, na.rm = TRUE) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = -log10(0.05), linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
  labs(title = "GSEA Volcano: Hallmark Pathways (V1 vs Control)",
       x = "Normalised Enrichment Score (NES)",
       y = "-log10(adjusted p-value)",
       color = NULL, size = "Pathway size") +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

This single plot replaces several rows of the dotplot and immediately shows two things: (a) the **immune / inflammatory pathways dominate the right-up corner** (activated and very significant); (b) any **suppressed** pathway (left-up) becomes visible — typically OxPhos / MYC targets — confirming the expected metabolic shift.

## 12.2c Validation: ORA ↔ GSEA agreement

GSEA and ORA answer slightly different questions (ranked-list shift vs DEG over-representation), but for a real biological signal they should **agree** on the top hits. Plotting ORA `-log10(p.adjust)` against GSEA `-log10(p.adjust)` for shared Hallmark IDs gives a direct validation: high concordance means our DEG calls are not a fluke.

```{r ora-gsea-concordance, fig.width=8, fig.height=6}
ora_v1_df  <- as.data.frame(ora_hallmark_v1) %>%
  dplyr::transmute(ID, ora_padj = p.adjust)
gsea_v1_df <- gsea_h_df %>%
  dplyr::transmute(ID, gsea_padj = p.adjust, NES)

concord <- dplyr::full_join(ora_v1_df, gsea_v1_df, by = "ID") %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    ora_padj  = ifelse(is.na(ora_padj),  1, ora_padj),
    gsea_padj = ifelse(is.na(gsea_padj), 1, gsea_padj),
    label     = gsub("HALLMARK_", "", ID),
    both_sig  = (ora_padj < 0.05) & (gsea_padj < 0.05)
  )

ggplot(concord, aes(x = -log10(ora_padj), y = -log10(gsea_padj))) +
  geom_point(aes(color = both_sig, size = abs(NES)), alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(`TRUE` = "firebrick", `FALSE` = "gray70"),
                     labels = c("Sig in only one or neither", "Sig in both"),
                     name = NULL) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = -log10(0.05), linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = -log10(0.05), linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
  geom_text_repel(data = concord %>% dplyr::filter(both_sig),
                  aes(label = label), size = 2.6, max.overlaps = 25) +
  labs(title = "ORA vs GSEA significance — Hallmark, V1 vs Control",
       subtitle = "Points above both dashed lines are significant in both methods (validation).",
       x = "-log10(ORA padj)", y = "-log10(GSEA padj)",
       size = "|NES|") +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

cat("\nHallmarks significant in both ORA and GSEA:",
    sum(concord$both_sig), "/", nrow(concord), "\n")
```

**Reading.** The diagonal is "perfect agreement". Top-right cluster contains the **validated** pathways: inflammatory response, TNFA-via-NFKB, IFN-γ response, complement, IL6-JAK-STAT3, etc. — these are detected by *both* methods, which is the strongest evidence the signal is real. Pathways significant only in GSEA (above the horizontal but to the left of the vertical line) are typical "subtle but coordinated" changes that ORA misses because few individual genes pass the |LFC| > 1, padj < 0.05 cutoff — this is exactly where GSEA earns its keep.

## 12.2d Robustness check: GSEA with LFC ranker vs signed −log10(p) ranker

The choice of ranking metric is one of the few subjective steps in GSEA. We run the same analysis with both rankers (Section 12.1) and check that the top hits agree. If both rankers produce the same Hallmark pathways at the top, the result is robust to the choice; if they disagree, the disagreeing pathways deserve extra scrutiny.

```{r gsea-ranker-compare-run, cache=TRUE}
gsea_v1_signedp <- GSEA(
  geneList     = ranked_lists_sigp$V1,
  TERM2GENE    = t2g_hallmark,
  pvalueCutoff = 1, eps = 0
)
```

```{r gsea-ranker-compare-plot, fig.width=8, fig.height=6}
df_lfc <- as.data.frame(gsea_hallmark_v1) %>%
  dplyr::transmute(ID, NES_lfc = NES, padj_lfc = p.adjust)
df_sgp <- as.data.frame(gsea_v1_signedp) %>%
  dplyr::transmute(ID, NES_sgp = NES, padj_sgp = p.adjust)

ranker_compare <- dplyr::full_join(df_lfc, df_sgp, by = "ID") %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    padj_lfc = ifelse(is.na(padj_lfc), 1, padj_lfc),
    padj_sgp = ifelse(is.na(padj_sgp), 1, padj_sgp),
    label    = gsub("HALLMARK_", "", ID),
    both_sig = padj_lfc < 0.05 & padj_sgp < 0.05
  )

cat("Pearson correlation between NES from the two rankers:",
    round(cor(ranker_compare$NES_lfc, ranker_compare$NES_sgp,
              use = "complete.obs"), 3), "\n")
cat("Hallmarks significant in BOTH rankers:",
    sum(ranker_compare$both_sig, na.rm = TRUE),
    "/", nrow(ranker_compare), "\n")

ggplot(ranker_compare, aes(x = NES_lfc, y = NES_sgp)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = both_sig), alpha = 0.85, size = 2.4) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(`TRUE` = "firebrick", `FALSE` = "gray70"),
                     labels = c("Sig in ≤1 ranker", "Sig in both"),
                     name = NULL) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dotted", color = "gray60") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dotted", color = "gray60") +
  geom_text_repel(data = ranker_compare %>% dplyr::filter(both_sig),
                  aes(label = label), size = 2.6, max.overlaps = 25) +
  labs(title = "GSEA NES — LFC ranker vs signed −log10(p) ranker (V1)",
       subtitle = "Diagonal = perfect agreement.",
       x = "NES (ranked by log2FoldChange)",
       y = "NES (ranked by signed −log10(p))") +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

**Reading.** A high correlation (typically >0.9 for strong biological signals) and a tight cluster of *both-significant* points along the diagonal mean the immune Hallmarks at the top are **not artefacts of the ranking choice**. Pathways that flip sign or change significance between the two rankers are the ones to be suspicious about; they tend to be small gene sets where a few high-LFC, low-power genes dominate one ranking but not the other. The rest of the GSEA analysis (Sections 12.3–12.4) uses the LFC ranker for consistency with everything downstream, but you can mention this robustness check in the discussion.

## 12.3 GSEA Enrichment Plots for Key Pathways

These "mountain plots" show how a pathway's member genes are distributed in the ranked list. The green line rises when it encounters pathway genes and falls otherwise. A peak at the **left** = upregulated; **right** =
downregulated.

```{r gsea-mountains, fig.height=4, fig.width=8, results='asis'}
key_pathways <- c("HALLMARK_INFLAMMATORY_RESPONSE",
                  "HALLMARK_TNFA_SIGNALING_VIA_NFKB",
                  "HALLMARK_INTERFERON_GAMMA_RESPONSE",
                  "HALLMARK_COMPLEMENT")

for (pw in key_pathways)
  if (pw %in% gsea_h_df$ID)
    print(gseaplot2(gsea_hallmark_v1, geneSetID = pw, title = pw))
```

## 12.4 GSEA Across All Time Points

```{r gsea-all-timepoints, cache=TRUE}
gsea_results <- list()
for (tp in names(ranked_lists))
  gsea_results[[tp]] <- GSEA(geneList = ranked_lists[[tp]],
                              TERM2GENE = t2g_hallmark,
                              pvalueCutoff = 1, eps = 0)
```

```{r nes-heatmap, fig.height=9, fig.width=7}
nes_all <- lapply(names(gsea_results), function(tp) {
  as.data.frame(gsea_results[[tp]]) %>%
    dplyr::select(ID, NES, p.adjust) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(timepoint = tp)
}) %>% dplyr::bind_rows()

# Keep pathways significant in at least one time point
sig_any <- nes_all %>% dplyr::filter(p.adjust < 0.05) %>%
  dplyr::pull(ID) %>% unique()

if (length(sig_any) > 0) {
  nes_mat <- nes_all %>%
    dplyr::filter(ID %in% sig_any) %>%
    dplyr::select(ID, NES, timepoint) %>%
    tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = timepoint, values_from = NES) %>%
    tibble::column_to_rownames("ID") %>% as.matrix()
  nes_mat[is.na(nes_mat)] <- 0

  pheatmap(nes_mat,
           color = colorRampPalette(c("steelblue","white","firebrick"))(100),
           cluster_cols = FALSE,
           main = "GSEA NES: Hallmark Pathways Across Time Points",
           fontsize_row = 7)
}
```

This heatmap shows how each pathway's activity (NES) evolves over time. **Red** = activated, **blue** = suppressed. Pathways that remain red from V1 through V5 represent the **sustained signature** described in the paper. Pathways that fade to white by V5 indicate resolution.

---
---

# 13. Biologic Theme Comparison

*Ref: Thomas's book Ch. 8.4.2*

`compareCluster` from clusterProfiler directly compares enrichment
results across multiple gene lists (our three time points), making the
temporal dynamics visually clear.

```{r compare-cluster, fig.height=10, fig.width=10}
gene_lists <- lapply(res_list, function(df) {
  df %>%
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(padj) & padj < 0.05 & abs(log2FoldChange) > 1 &
                  !is.na(Symbol) & Symbol != "") %>%
    dplyr::pull(Symbol) %>% unique()
})

cc_result <- compareCluster(
  geneClusters = gene_lists,
  fun = "enricher",
  TERM2GENE = t2g_hallmark,
  pvalueCutoff = 0.05, pAdjustMethod = "BH"
)

if (nrow(as.data.frame(cc_result)) > 0)
  dotplot(cc_result) +
    ggtitle("Biologic Theme Comparison: Hallmark Across Time Points") +
    theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```
Each column is a time point, each row is a pathway. Dot size = number of DEGs in that pathway; colour = adjusted p-value. This directly shows which pathways are shared across time points and which are specific to the acute phase (V1 only).


---
---

# 14. 2nd Generation (cont.): GSVA (Gene Set Variation Analysis)

*Ref: Thomas's book Ch. 8.1.2.2, 8.4.5; Hänzelmann et al. 2013*

GSVA completes the 2nd generation of pathway analysis. Unlike GSEA
(which compares two groups), GSVA computes a **per-sample pathway
score**. This preserves the full sample structure, so we can test
pathway differences with standard linear models -- exactly as Thomas
describes in Ch. 8.4.5.

Think of it this way:

- **ORA** (1st gen): "Which pathways have too many DEGs?" (binary: gene is DE or not)
- **GSEA** (2nd gen-a): "Do pathway genes cluster at the top/bottom of the ranked list?" (uses fold-change magnitude, but still compares two groups)
- **GSVA** (2nd gen-b): "For each sample individually, how active is this pathway?" (produces a score per sample per pathway -- then you can run any statistical model on those scores)

## 14.1 Prepare Gene Sets as Named Lists

GSVA needs gene sets as a named list of character vectors (gene symbols).

```{r gsva-genesets}
hallmark_sets <- msigdb_hallmark %>%
  dplyr::select(gs_name, gene_symbol) %>%
  dplyr::group_by(gs_name) %>%
  dplyr::summarise(genes = list(unique(gene_symbol)), .groups = "drop") %>%
  tibble::deframe()

cat("Hallmark gene sets:", length(hallmark_sets), "\n")
cat("Example set sizes:", head(sapply(hallmark_sets, length)), "\n")
```

## 14.2 Prepare Expression Matrix with Gene Symbols

GSVA needs the matrix rows to be gene symbols (matching the gene sets),
not Entrez IDs.

```{r gsva-matrix}
# Use VST-normalised data for GSVA (Gaussian kernel, Ch. 8.4.5)
vst_mat <- assay(vsd)

# Map Entrez IDs to Symbols
symbol_map <- gene_annot$Symbol[match(as.numeric(rownames(vst_mat)),
                                       gene_annot$GeneID)]

# Keep only rows with valid symbols, handle duplicates by keeping highest mean
keep_sym <- !is.na(symbol_map) & symbol_map != ""
vst_sym  <- vst_mat[keep_sym, ]
rownames(vst_sym) <- symbol_map[keep_sym]

# Remove duplicate gene symbols (keep the one with highest mean expression)
dup_idx <- duplicated(rownames(vst_sym))
vst_sym <- vst_sym[!dup_idx, ]

cat("GSVA input matrix:", nrow(vst_sym), "genes x", ncol(vst_sym), "samples\n")
```

## 14.3 Run GSVA

```{r run-gsva, cache=TRUE}
gsva_param <- gsvaParam(
  exprData = vst_sym,
  geneSets = hallmark_sets,
  kcdf     = "Gaussian",   # Gaussian for continuous (VST) data; Poisson for counts
  maxDiff  = TRUE
)

gsva_scores <- gsva(gsva_param)

cat("GSVA output:", nrow(gsva_scores), "pathways x", ncol(gsva_scores), "samples\n")
```

## 14.4 GSVA Heatmap

Each cell shows how active a pathway is in a given sample (relative
to other samples). This reveals the per-patient, per-time-point
pathway landscape.

```{r gsva-heatmap, fig.height=12, fig.width=10}
pheatmap(gsva_scores,
         annotation_col = row_annot,
         annotation_colors = ann_colors,
         show_colnames = FALSE,
         fontsize_row = 6,
         color = colorRampPalette(c("steelblue", "white", "firebrick"))(100),
         main = "GSVA Scores: Hallmark Pathways (per sample)",
         clustering_distance_rows = "correlation",
         cutree_cols = 4)
```

The key advantage of GSVA over GSEA: you can see individual patient
variation. Some V5 patients may still have highly activated
inflammatory pathways (potential PTLDS candidates), while others
return to control-like levels.

## 14.5 Test GSVA Scores Between Groups

Following Thomas's Ch. 8.4.5: since GSVA scores are continuous values
per sample, we can use a standard linear model (limma) to test which
pathways differ significantly between groups. This mirrors the workflow
from Ch. 7.5.1.

```{r gsva-limma}
library(limma)

design_gsva <- model.matrix(~ 0 + group, data = annot)
colnames(design_gsva) <- levels(annot$group)

# Fit linear model to GSVA scores (instead of gene expression)
fit_gsva <- lmFit(gsva_scores, design_gsva)

# Define contrasts (same comparisons as DESeq2)
contrast_mat <- makeContrasts(
  V1_vs_Ctrl = Lyme_V1 - Control,
  V2_vs_Ctrl = Lyme_V2 - Control,
  V5_vs_Ctrl = Lyme_V5 - Control,
  levels = design_gsva
)

fit_gsva2 <- contrasts.fit(fit_gsva, contrast_mat) %>% eBayes()
```

```{r gsva-results}
gsva_results_list <- list()
for (coef_name in colnames(contrast_mat)) {
  tt <- topTable(fit_gsva2, coef = coef_name, number = Inf) %>%
    tibble::rownames_to_column("pathway")
  gsva_results_list[[coef_name]] <- tt
  
  n_sig <- sum(tt$adj.P.Val < 0.05)
  cat(coef_name, ":", n_sig, "pathways with adj.P.Val < 0.05\n")
}
```

## 14.6 GSVA Volcano-Style Plot

```{r gsva-volcano, fig.width=16, fig.height=6}
gsva_volcano_plots <- lapply(names(gsva_results_list), function(nm) {
  df <- gsva_results_list[[nm]] %>%
    dplyr::mutate(
      sig = ifelse(adj.P.Val < 0.05, "Significant", "NS"),
      label = ifelse(adj.P.Val < 0.05,
                     gsub("HALLMARK_", "", pathway), NA)
    )
  ggplot(df, aes(x = logFC, y = -log10(P.Value), color = sig)) +
    geom_point(size = 2.5) +
    scale_color_manual(values = c(Significant = "firebrick", NS = "gray60")) +
    geom_text_repel(aes(label = label), size = 2.2, max.overlaps = 20,
                    na.rm = TRUE) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = -log10(0.05), linetype = "dashed") +
    labs(title = gsub("_", " ", nm),
         x = "GSVA score difference", y = "-log10(p-value)") +
    theme(legend.position = "none")
})
```
