```{r gsva-volcano, fig.width=16, fig.height=6}
grid.arrange(grobs = gsva_volcano_plots, ncol = 3)
```

Each dot is a Hallmark pathway. The x-axis is the difference in GSVA
score between Lyme and Control (positive = more active in Lyme).
Labeled pathways are significant (adj.P.Val < 0.05). Compare the
three panels: V1 should have the most extreme differences, V5 the
least -- mirroring the gene-level DEG pattern.

## 14.7 GSVA Score Boxplots for Key Pathways

```{r gsva-boxplots, fig.height=10, fig.width=12}
key_pw <- c("HALLMARK_INFLAMMATORY_RESPONSE",
            "HALLMARK_TNFA_SIGNALING_VIA_NFKB",
            "HALLMARK_INTERFERON_GAMMA_RESPONSE",
            "HALLMARK_COMPLEMENT",
            "HALLMARK_IL6_JAK_STAT3_SIGNALING",
            "HALLMARK_APOPTOSIS")

gsva_long <- gsva_scores[rownames(gsva_scores) %in% key_pw, ] %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  tibble::rownames_to_column("pathway") %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(-pathway, names_to = "GSM", values_to = "score") %>%
  dplyr::left_join(annot %>% dplyr::select(GSM, group, visit), by = "GSM") %>%
  dplyr::mutate(pathway = gsub("HALLMARK_", "", pathway))

ggplot(gsva_long, aes(x = group, y = score, fill = group)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.6, outlier.shape = NA) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.15, size = 1.5, alpha = 0.6) +
  facet_wrap(~ pathway, scales = "free_y", ncol = 3) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "GSVA Scores for Key Immune Pathways",
       subtitle = "Each dot is a patient. Higher = more pathway activity in that sample.",
       x = NULL, y = "GSVA Score") +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        strip.text = element_text(size = 8))
```

These boxplots are the unique output of GSVA that neither ORA nor GSEA
can produce. Each dot is an individual patient's pathway activity.
Look for:

- **V1 >> Control**: Pathway is activated during acute infection (expected for immune/inflammatory pathways).
- **V2 still elevated**: Pathway stays active even after antibiotic treatment -- evidence for the sustained signature.
- **V5 approaching Control**: Pathway is resolving (good sign for most patients).
- **V5 patients still elevated**: These outlier dots may correspond to patients with persistent symptoms (PTLDS).

This connects directly to the paper's central finding: the gene expression
signature of Lyme disease persists after treatment. GSVA lets us see
this at the **individual patient level**, which the original study's
bulk comparisons could not show.

---
---

# 15. Third Generation: SPIA (topology-aware KEGG perturbation)

*Ref: Khatri et al. 2012 (three generations); Tarca et al. 2009; Thomas's book Ch. 8 — pathway topology*

**What “third generation” means here.** First-generation ORA asks whether a pathway contains more DEG labels than expected. Second-generation GSEA/GSVA uses ranked scores or per-sample enrichment without explicit **wiring** between genes. **Signalling Pathway Impact Analysis (SPIA)** adds a *perturbation* term: it walks curated **KEGG** signalling topology so that genes upstream/downstream and activation vs inhibition relations contribute differently from a flat hypergeometric count. The combined evidence (over-representation **pNDE** + topology **pPERT**) yields a global **pG** (and FDR-style **pGFdr**).

### Should we trust SPIA for this course project?

SPIA is pedagogically valuable: it makes the “topology” idea concrete and produces interpretable **Activated / Inhibited** status per pathway. For a *scientific* discussion with your professor, consider these limitations:

- **Database scope:** SPIA is tied to **KEGG** pathway maps (not MSigDB Hallmark). Immune–infection biology is only partially captured; many Lyme-relevant processes appear in Hallmark/GO instead.
- **Model assumptions:** Edge signs and “perturbation accumulation” are approximations; real immune networks are context- and cell-type–specific.
- **Redundancy:** Top SPIA hits may overlap messages already seen in ORA/GSEA/GSVA — agreement strengthens evidence; disagreement invites caution, not automatic dismissal.
- **Licensing / maintenance:** KEGG data are periodically updated; results can shift slightly between SPIA/database versions.

We run SPIA **anyway** so you can compare outputs side-by-side with first- and second-generation results and document the workflow.

## 15.1 Prepare DE vectors (Entrez) and run SPIA

SPIA expects a **named numeric vector of log2 fold changes for differentially expressed genes** (Entrez IDs as names) and the **universe** of all Entrez IDs tested (`rownames(dds)`).

```{r spia-run, cache=TRUE}
spia_from_res <- function(res_df, dds_obj) {
  sig <- res_df %>%
    dplyr::filter(!is.na(padj), padj < 0.05, !is.na(log2FoldChange)) %>%
    dplyr::mutate(GeneID = as.character(GeneID))
  if (nrow(sig) < 5)
    return(NULL)
  # one value per Entrez: keep strongest |LFC| if duplicates exist
  sig <- sig %>%
    dplyr::group_by(GeneID) %>%
    dplyr::slice_max(order_by = abs(log2FoldChange), n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
    dplyr::ungroup()
  de <- sig$log2FoldChange
  names(de) <- sig$GeneID
  all_ids <- as.character(rownames(dds_obj))
  spia(de, all_ids, organism = "hsa", nB = 2000, verbose = FALSE)
}

spia_v1 <- spia_from_res(res_list$V1, dds)
spia_v2 <- spia_from_res(res_list$V2, dds)
spia_v5 <- spia_from_res(res_list$V5, dds)
```

## 15.2 Top KEGG pathways (global pG)

```{r spia-tables}
fmt_spia <- function(x, n = 12) {
  if (is.null(x) || nrow(x) == 0)
    return(invisible(NULL))
  tibble::rownames_to_column(as.data.frame(x), "pathway_id") %>%
    dplyr::arrange(pG) %>%
    dplyr::select(Name, ID, Status, pNDE, pPERT, pG, pGFdr) %>%
    head(n)
}

fmt_spia(spia_v1)
fmt_spia(spia_v2)
fmt_spia(spia_v5)
```

## 15.3 Visualize top hits (V1)

```{r spia-plot-v1, fig.height=6}
if (!is.null(spia_v1) && nrow(spia_v1) > 0) {
  top <- tibble::rownames_to_column(as.data.frame(spia_v1), "pathway_id") %>%
    dplyr::arrange(pG) %>%
    head(15)
  ggplot(top, aes(x = reorder(Name, -log10(pG)), y = -log10(pG), fill = Status)) +
    geom_col() +
    coord_flip() +
    scale_fill_manual(values = c(Activated = "firebrick", Inhibited = "steelblue",
                                 Mixed = "gray50"),
                      na.value = "gray70") +
    labs(title = "SPIA: top KEGG pathways (V1 vs Control)",
         subtitle = "Bar height = -log10(global pG). Status from SPIA.",
         x = NULL, y = "-log10(pG)") +
    theme(legend.position = "bottom")
}
```

Pathways with large **|tA|** (total net accumulation) and small **pPERT** are those where **topology** contributes strongly, not just list overlap.

## 15.4 Does SPIA agree with the Hallmark / GO results? (cross-database validation)

**Short answer:** SPIA uses a *different reference database* (KEGG signalling maps) than ORA/GSEA/GSVA in Sections 11–14 (which use **MSigDB Hallmark** + **GO:BP**). Pathway *names* therefore do not match 1-to-1 — but we can still check whether the same biological signal is captured by all three databases.

### Why a direct name-match doesn't work

| Database | Source | Granularity | Used by |
|----------|--------|-------------|---------|
| **MSigDB Hallmark** | Liberzon *et al.* 2015 — 50 curated, low-redundancy gene sets | broad biological themes (~200 genes / set) | ORA, GSEA, GSVA in Sections 11–14 |
| **GO:BP** | Gene Ontology Consortium | thousands of overlapping terms | ORA in Section 11.3 |
| **KEGG signalling maps** | Kanehisa Lab | ~330 curated pathways with **edges** (activation/inhibition) | SPIA in this section |

There is overlap *in the underlying biology* (e.g. "Cytokine-cytokine receptor interaction" in KEGG ↔ `HALLMARK_INFLAMMATORY_RESPONSE`) but the gene sets are not identical — KEGG is more mechanistic and signalling-focused, Hallmark is more thematic.

### Strategy

The cleanest validation is **same database, multiple methods**: run **ORA-KEGG** and **GSEA-KEGG** on V1 using KEGG gene sets pulled from MSigDB, then compare to **SPIA**. Any disagreement is then *methodological* (topology vs. flat enrichment), not *database-driven*. We then add a **gene-level** overlap with the Hallmark leading edge to bridge to Sections 11–12.

### 15.4.1 Pull KEGG gene sets from MSigDB

```{r kegg-genesets, cache=FALSE}
msigdb_kegg <- msigdbr(species = "Homo sapiens", collection = "C2") %>%
  dplyr::filter(grepl("^KEGG_", gs_name))

cat("KEGG gene sets pulled from MSigDB:",
    length(unique(msigdb_kegg$gs_name)), "pathways\n")

t2g_kegg <- msigdb_kegg %>% dplyr::select(gs_name, gene_symbol)
```

### 15.4.2 KEGG ORA + KEGG GSEA on V1

```{r kegg-ora-gsea, cache=TRUE}
ora_kegg_v1 <- enricher(
  gene = sig_sym_v1, universe = univ_sym,
  TERM2GENE = t2g_kegg,
  pvalueCutoff = 1, pAdjustMethod = "BH", qvalueCutoff = 1
)

gsea_kegg_v1 <- GSEA(
  geneList     = ranked_lists$V1,
  TERM2GENE    = t2g_kegg,
  pvalueCutoff = 1, eps = 0
)

cat("KEGG ORA  significant pathways (p.adjust<0.05):",
    sum(as.data.frame(ora_kegg_v1)$p.adjust  < 0.05), "\n")
cat("KEGG GSEA significant pathways (p.adjust<0.05):",
    sum(as.data.frame(gsea_kegg_v1)$p.adjust < 0.05), "\n")
```

### 15.4.3 Map SPIA → MSigDB-KEGG and merge

SPIA exposes the human-readable pathway name (e.g. `"Cytokine-cytokine receptor interaction"`); MSigDB exposes the upper-snake-case `gs_name` (e.g. `"KEGG_CYTOKINE_CYTOKINE_RECEPTOR_INTERACTION"`). We normalise both to the same form and join.

```{r spia-kegg-mapping}
norm_kegg_name <- function(s) {
  s <- toupper(s)
  s <- gsub("[^A-Z0-9]+", "_", s)
  s <- gsub("^_+|_+$", "", s)
  s
}

spia_v1_df <- tibble::rownames_to_column(as.data.frame(spia_v1), "spia_pwid") %>%
  dplyr::transmute(
    spia_pwid,
    pathway_name = Name,
    norm         = norm_kegg_name(Name),
    spia_status  = Status,
    spia_pG      = pG,
    spia_pGFdr   = pGFdr
  )

ora_kegg_df <- as.data.frame(ora_kegg_v1) %>%
  dplyr::transmute(
    norm      = sub("^KEGG_", "", ID),
    ora_padj  = p.adjust,
    ora_count = Count
  )

gsea_kegg_df <- as.data.frame(gsea_kegg_v1) %>%
  dplyr::transmute(
    norm      = sub("^KEGG_", "", ID),
    gsea_padj = p.adjust,
    gsea_NES  = NES
  )

cross_kegg <- spia_v1_df %>%
  dplyr::left_join(ora_kegg_df,  by = "norm") %>%
  dplyr::left_join(gsea_kegg_df, by = "norm")

n_matched <- sum(!is.na(cross_kegg$gsea_padj) | !is.na(cross_kegg$ora_padj))
cat("SPIA pathways successfully mapped to MSigDB KEGG:",
    n_matched, "/", nrow(cross_kegg), "\n")
```

```{r spia-cross-table}
top_cross <- cross_kegg %>%
  dplyr::arrange(spia_pG) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    spia_pG    = signif(spia_pG, 2),
    spia_pGFdr = signif(spia_pGFdr, 2),
    ora_padj   = signif(ora_padj, 2),
    gsea_padj  = signif(gsea_padj, 2),
    gsea_NES   = round(gsea_NES, 2)
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(pathway_name, spia_status,
                spia_pG, spia_pGFdr,
                ora_padj, ora_count,
                gsea_NES, gsea_padj) %>%
  head(15)

knitr::kable(top_cross, caption = "Top 15 SPIA pathways at V1, with KEGG-ORA and KEGG-GSEA evidence side-by-side. Empty cells = pathway present in SPIA but not in MSigDB's KEGG collection (or vice versa).")
```

### 15.4.4 Visualise the agreement

A single scatter showing every KEGG pathway that *both* SPIA and KEGG-GSEA could score: x-axis = SPIA evidence (`-log10(pG)`), y-axis = GSEA evidence (`-log10(p.adjust)`). Pathways that are significant in *both* (top-right) are the most robust hits — agreement across two independent methods on the same database.

```{r spia-vs-gsea-kegg, fig.width=8, fig.height=6}
plot_df <- cross_kegg %>%
  dplyr::filter(!is.na(spia_pG) & !is.na(gsea_padj)) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    sig_spia = spia_pGFdr < 0.05,
    sig_gsea = gsea_padj  < 0.05,
    both     = sig_spia & sig_gsea,
    label    = ifelse(both, pathway_name, NA)
  )

ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = -log10(spia_pG), y = -log10(gsea_padj))) +
  geom_point(aes(color = both, size = abs(gsea_NES)), alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(`TRUE` = "firebrick", `FALSE` = "gray70"),
                     labels = c("Sig in only one", "Sig in both"),
                     name   = NULL) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = -log10(0.05), linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = -log10(0.05), linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40") +
  geom_text_repel(aes(label = label), size = 2.6, max.overlaps = 25, na.rm = TRUE) +
  labs(title = "SPIA vs KEGG-GSEA — KEGG pathways, V1 vs Control",
       subtitle = "Same database (KEGG), two methods. Top-right = robust.",
       x = "-log10(SPIA pG)",
       y = "-log10(GSEA padj)",
       size = "|GSEA NES|") +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

### 15.4.5 Bridge back to Hallmark — *gene-level* agreement

Hallmark and KEGG are different gene-set definitions, so a pathway-name comparison is meaningless. The cleanest bridge is at the **gene level**: take the **leading-edge genes** from each top-5 Hallmark in GSEA (Section 12.2) and intersect them with the **member genes** of each top-5 SPIA KEGG pathway. A high overlap means SPIA and Hallmark are pointing to the same biology even if they disagree on the pathway label.

```{r spia-hallmark-gene-overlap, fig.width=9, fig.height=5}
top5_hallmark <- gsea_h_df %>%
  dplyr::arrange(p.adjust) %>%
  head(5)
top5_hallmark_genes <- setNames(
  strsplit(top5_hallmark$core_enrichment, "/"),
  gsub("HALLMARK_", "", top5_hallmark$ID)
)

top5_spia <- cross_kegg %>%
  dplyr::arrange(spia_pG) %>%
  head(5)
kegg_members <- split(msigdb_kegg$gene_symbol, msigdb_kegg$gs_name)
top5_spia_genes <- lapply(top5_spia$norm, function(n) {
  key <- paste0("KEGG_", n)
  if (key %in% names(kegg_members)) unique(kegg_members[[key]]) else character(0)
})
names(top5_spia_genes) <- top5_spia$pathway_name

overlap_mat <- outer(
  seq_along(top5_hallmark_genes),
  seq_along(top5_spia_genes),
  Vectorize(function(i, j) {
    a <- top5_hallmark_genes[[i]]; b <- top5_spia_genes[[j]]
    if (length(a) == 0 || length(b) == 0) return(0)
    length(intersect(a, b))
  })
)
rownames(overlap_mat) <- names(top5_hallmark_genes)
colnames(overlap_mat) <- names(top5_spia_genes)

overlap_long <- as.data.frame(as.table(overlap_mat))
colnames(overlap_long) <- c("Hallmark", "SPIA_KEGG", "shared_genes")

ggplot(overlap_long, aes(x = SPIA_KEGG, y = Hallmark, fill = shared_genes)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = shared_genes), size = 3.5) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "firebrick") +
  labs(title = "Shared genes — top-5 Hallmark (GSEA leading edge) vs top-5 SPIA KEGG (full pathway)",
       x = "SPIA top KEGG pathways", y = "Hallmark top pathways", fill = "Shared\ngenes") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1, size = 8),
        axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8))
```

**Reading the heatmap.** Cells with high counts (red) tell us a SPIA-flagged KEGG pathway and a GSEA-flagged Hallmark are describing **the same population of genes**. For acute Lyme we expect strong overlap between e.g. `INFLAMMATORY_RESPONSE` ↔ `Cytokine-cytokine receptor interaction` and `IFN_GAMMA_RESPONSE` ↔ `Toll-like receptor signaling` / `JAK-STAT signaling`. If those cells are highly populated, the answer is "**Yes, SPIA agrees with the Hallmark/GSEA result at the gene level — it is just dressing the same biology in KEGG vocabulary.**"

### 15.4.6 Save the cross-database table

```{r save-spia-cross}
write.csv(cross_kegg %>% dplyr::arrange(spia_pG),
          file.path(results_dir, "Cross_DB_SPIA_vs_KEGG_ORA_GSEA_V1.csv"),
          row.names = FALSE)
cat("Saved:", file.path(results_dir, "Cross_DB_SPIA_vs_KEGG_ORA_GSEA_V1.csv"), "\n")
```

---
---

# 16. Save All Results

```{r save-all-results}
for (tp in names(ora_hallmark_all)) {
  df <- as.data.frame(ora_hallmark_all[[tp]])
  if (nrow(df) > 0) write.csv(df, file.path(results_dir,
    paste0("ORA_Hallmark_", tp, ".csv")), row.names = FALSE)
}
for (tp in names(gsea_results)) {
  df <- as.data.frame(gsea_results[[tp]])
  if (nrow(df) > 0) write.csv(df, file.path(results_dir,
    paste0("GSEA_Hallmark_", tp, ".csv")), row.names = FALSE)
}
for (nm in names(gsva_results_list)) {
  write.csv(gsva_results_list[[nm]], file.path(results_dir,
    paste0("GSVA_Hallmark_", nm, ".csv")), row.names = FALSE)
}
write.csv(gsva_scores, file.path(results_dir, "GSVA_scores_per_sample.csv"))

if (!is.null(spia_v1) && nrow(spia_v1) > 0)
  write.csv(as.data.frame(spia_v1), file.path(results_dir, "SPIA_KEGG_V1_vs_Control.csv"),
            row.names = TRUE)
if (!is.null(spia_v2) && nrow(spia_v2) > 0)
  write.csv(as.data.frame(spia_v2), file.path(results_dir, "SPIA_KEGG_V2_vs_Control.csv"),
            row.names = TRUE)
if (!is.null(spia_v5) && nrow(spia_v5) > 0)
  write.csv(as.data.frame(spia_v5), file.path(results_dir, "SPIA_KEGG_V5_vs_Control.csv"),
            row.names = TRUE)

cat("All results saved to:", results_dir, "\n")
```

---
---

# 17. Results Interpretation & Discussion

This section brings together all findings into a coherent narrative.

## 17.1 What did we find? (The Big Picture)

```{r interpretation-summary}
cat("DATA\n","  Genome reference: GRCh38.p13 (upgraded from hg19)\n"
,"  Aligner: Kallisto (upgraded from TopHat2/Cufflinks2)\n",
"  Samples analysed:", ncol(counts_filtered), "\n",
"  Genes after filtering:", nrow(counts_filtered), "\n\n",
"DIFFERENTIAL EXPRESSION (DESeq2, |lfc|>1, padj<0.05)\n")
print(deg_summary)

cat("\nOriginal study (Limma/Voom on FPKM, |FC|>1.5, FDR<0.1%):\n",
"  V1: 1,235 DEGs | V2: 1,060 DEGs | V5: 686 DEGs\n\n",
"PATHWAY ANALYSIS (ORA + GSEA/GSVA + SPIA)\n",
"  1st gen ORA  (Hallmark, V1):", nrow(as.data.frame(ora_hallmark_v1)),
    "enriched pathways\n",
"  1st gen ORA  (GO:BP, V1):  ",
    sum(as.data.frame(ora_gobp_v1)$p.adjust < 0.05), "enriched terms\n")
cat("  2nd gen GSEA (Hallmark, V1):", sum(gsea_h_df$p.adjust < 0.05),
    "significant pathways\n",
"  2nd gen GSVA (Hallmark, V1):",
    sum(gsva_results_list$V1_vs_Ctrl$adj.P.Val < 0.05),
    "differentially active pathways\n")
if (!is.null(spia_v1) && nrow(spia_v1) > 0) {
  cat("  3rd gen SPIA (KEGG, V1):    ",
      sum(spia_v1$pGFdr < 0.05, na.rm = TRUE),
      "pathways with pGFdr < 0.05\n")
} else {
  cat("  3rd gen SPIA (KEGG, V1):    not computed or empty\n")
}
```

## 17.2 Biological Interpretation

### The Acute Phase (V1): A Strong Inflammatory Storm

At V1 (acute Lyme diagnosis, before antibiotics) we detect the largest number of DEGs and the most enriched pathways. ORA, GSEA and GSVA all converge on the same five Hallmark pathways at the top — and the cross-method validation in **Section 12.2c** confirms they are not method-specific artefacts. The dominant biological themes are:

- **TNFA signaling via NF-κB** — *master pro-inflammatory programme*. The full mechanism (TNF-α → TNFR1 → IKK → NF-κB → autocrine cytokine loop), the leading-edge gene table, and the cytokine glossary are detailed in **Section 11.2.1**. Original paper also called out TNF-α as a top upstream regulator.
- **Interferon-γ response** — IFN-γ from T cells and NK cells activates macrophages and drives Th1 immunity (the lineage required against spirochetal pathogens). Matches the original study's TREM1-mediated Th1 activation finding. The core ISGs *ISG15 / IFI27 / BATF2* (highlighted in **Section 10.6**) explain why this Hallmark scores so high.
- **Inflammatory response** — broad cytokine, chemokine, and leukocyte-recruitment programme; expected hallmark of an acute bacterial infection.
- **Complement** — ancient innate defence that lyses bacteria and tags them for phagocytosis. *SERPING1* (a complement regulator highlighted in 10.6) sits in the leading edge.
- **IL6–JAK–STAT3 signaling** — IL-6 drives the acute-phase response (CRP, fever); identified as a top upstream regulator at V1 and V2 in the original paper.

The gene-level story (**Section 10.6**) maps cleanly onto these pathway-level themes:

- **Pathogen sensing.** *TLR2* (Section 10.6, pick #4) is the direct *B. burgdorferi* lipoprotein receptor — the molecular entry point that lights up the entire downstream NF-κB / IFN-γ programme described above.
- **Innate effector activation.** *S100A8 / S100A9* (calprotectin, pick #2) and *FCGR1A* (CD64, pick #3) reflect massive neutrophil + monocyte activation, which is what the *Inflammatory Response* and *Complement* Hallmarks summarise at the gene-set level.
- **Persistent biomarker.** *CXCL10* (pick #1) is the single most cited Lyme/PTLDS biomarker; its strong V1 induction is the gene-level driver of *Interferon-γ Response* and *IL6-JAK-STAT3*, and it carries forward into V2/V5 (see below).
- **Resolution / exhaustion machinery already engaged.** *IDO1* (pick #5) and *CD274 / PD-L1* (pick #6) are induced even at V1, hinting that the host is already activating immunoregulatory brakes — relevant for why the signature later fails to fully resolve.

**SPIA (Section 15)** adds the topology-aware view: pathways where both list enrichment *and* signed graph perturbation suggest activation/inhibition (e.g. chemokine signalling, lysosome, cell-cycle KEGG maps). Treat as complementary to Hallmark/GO, not a one-to-one match.

### Post-Treatment (V2): The Signature Persists

At V2 (3 weeks later, after completing antibiotics) a substantial number of DEGs and Hallmark pathways remain active — the paper's central finding: **the gene-expression signature does not promptly return to baseline after treatment**, unlike most other acute infections.

The NES heatmap (**Section 12.4**) and GSVA boxplots (**Section 14.7**) show that most inflammatory Hallmarks (TNFA-via-NFKB, Inflammatory Response, IFN-γ Response — see 11.2.1 and 10.6 for what those *mean*) stay elevated at V2 with reduced but non-zero intensity. The mechanism implied by the genes in 10.6 is consistent with this: even after the bacterium is cleared, the *NF-κB feedback loop* documented in 11.2.1 has run for weeks, induced its own self-sustaining cytokine pool (IL-6, IL-1, TNF), and only relaxes slowly. The IDO1 / PD-L1 brakes seen at V1 are now actively trying to dampen the response, but incompletely.

### Six Months Later (V5): Partial but Incomplete Recovery

By V5 (6 months post-treatment) the number of DEGs drops substantially but does not reach zero, and a residual subset of inflammatory pathways still register as differentially active. The **per-sample GSVA boxplots** (Section 14.7) are the most informative view here: most V5 patients approach control levels, but **a minority of outlier samples remain elevated** — these are the candidate PTLDS cases.

The original paper found 686 DEGs at V5 and noted 31–60% pathway overlap with chronic autoimmune diseases (SLE, RA). Our re-analysis is consistent with that: a lingering transcriptomic perturbation at 6 months, driven by the same TNF-α/NF-κB and IFN-γ programmes from V1 (see 11.2.1) — just at lower intensity and now restricted to a subset of patients.

If we wanted *one gene to track* across V1 → V2 → V5 in a future serum-based clinical follow-up, **CXCL10 (pick #1 in Section 10.6)** is the strongest candidate, with prior independent validation as a PTLDS biomarker (Soloski et al. 2014, Aucott et al. 2016).

## 17.3 Answering Open Questions from the Analysis

**Section 5.2 -- "Is the mean set correctly for the mean-variance plot?"**
Yes. The plot correctly shows that for raw RNA-Seq counts, the variance
consistently exceeds the mean (overdispersion). The red Poisson line
(variance = mean) is a theoretical baseline. The fact that points sit
above it is not an error; it is the fundamental property of count data
that justifies using the negative binomial model in DESeq2.

**Section 7.4 -- "VST doesn't seem to flatten the mean-SD relationship?"**
The VST does reduce the dependency, even if visually the "before" and
"after" plots look similar. The key metric is the correlation between
mean and SD: even a modest reduction (e.g., from 0.8 to 0.5) meaningfully
improves downstream analyses like PCA. The fact that the correlation
doesn't drop to zero is normal -- VST is an approximation, not magic.
For small sample sizes, `rlog()` (regularised log) can perform better
than VST but is much slower. Both are acceptable for this project.

**MA plots (Sections 5.3, 7.3, 10.1b) — do they validate normalization?**  
Section 5.3 uses raw log2 counts: systematic offset is expected. Section 7.3 uses **size-factor–normalized** counts only; the loess trend should sit closer to **M = 0**. Section **10.1b** uses **DESeq2**'s `plotMA()` after dispersion shrinkage: the central mass of points at moderate-to-high expression should lie near **LFC = 0** for non-DEGs (blue = padj &lt; alpha). Together, these three plots answer "did we fix depth?" vs "what does the fitted model say about differential expression?"

**Section 12.2 -- "Which pathways should we expect activated/suppressed?"**
Based on what we know about Lyme disease biology:

*Expected activated:* Inflammatory response, TNF-alpha signaling,
interferon signaling (IFN-gamma, IFN-alpha), complement, IL6-JAK-STAT,
TLR signaling, NF-kB signaling, apoptosis.

*Potentially suppressed:* The original paper found eIF2 signaling
(translation initiation) downregulated at all time points. In our GSEA
results, look for pathways with negative NES (blue in the heatmap).
Suppressed MYC targets or mTORC1 signaling would indicate reduced
cell growth/proliferation, consistent with immune cells diverting
resources from growth to defence.

## 17.4 Book Chapter Reference Map

| Our Section | Thomas's Book Chapter | Method |
|-------------|-----------------------|--------|
| 4.1 Library sizes | Ch. 7.2 | Quality Control / depth imbalance check |
| 4.3 RLE plot | Ch. 7.2.4 | Relative Log Expression |
| 5.3 MA plot | Ch. 7.2.7 | Mean-difference plot |
| 7.2 Size factors | Ch. 7.6 | Median-of-ratios normalisation (handles depth imbalance from 4.1) |
| 7. DESeq2 | Ch. 7.6 | Differential expression |
| 8. PCA | Ch. 7.2.2 | Dimensionality reduction |
| 9. Distance heatmap | Ch. 7.2.1 | Sample distance |
| 10.1a Dispersion | Ch. 7.6 | Quantifies dataset variability |
| 10.1b / 7.3 / 5.3 MA | Ch. 7.2.7 | Mean–difference / DESeq2 MA |
| 10.6 Genes of interest | n/a | Literature-curated picks for discussion |
| 11. ORA | Ch. 8.1.1, 8.4.1 | 1st generation pathway analysis |
| 11.2.1 TNFA-via-NFKB | Ch. 8.2 | Detailed pathway explanation |
| 12. GSEA | Ch. 8.1.2.1, 8.4.3 | 2nd generation pathway analysis |
| 12.2b GSEA volcano | Ch. 8.4.3 | Pathway-level volcano (NES vs adj.p) |
| 12.2c ORA ↔ GSEA | Ch. 8 | Cross-method validation |
| 13. compareCluster | Ch. 8.4.2 | Biologic theme comparison |
| 14. GSVA | Ch. 8.1.2.2, 8.4.5 | 2nd generation (per-sample scores) |
| 15. SPIA | Ch. 8 (topology); Khatri et al. 2012 | 3rd generation (KEGG perturbation) |
| MSigDB usage | Ch. 8.2 | Molecular Signature Database |

---

# Session Info

```{r session-info}
sessionInfo()
```
